# Advancing Continuous Manufacturing in Pharmaceutical Production

The pharmaceutical industry stands at a pivotal moment as manufacturers increasingly adopt continuous manufacturing processes. At BioNova Global Headquarters, we've observed significant shifts in how companies approach drug production over the past five years. Continuous manufacturing offers distinct advantages over traditional batch processes, including improved product consistency, reduced contamination risk, and substantially lower operational costs. For facilities managing multiple product lines, the flexibility of continuous systems enables faster transitions between compounds while maintaining stringent quality standards.

Implementing continuous manufacturing requires careful consideration of equipment integration and process control systems. Our engineering teams work closely with regulatory consultants to ensure that continuous processes meet FDA expectations for Process Analytical Technology (PAT) and real-time release testing. The investment in automated control systems and advanced sensors provides manufacturers with unprecedented visibility into critical process parameters, allowing for immediate adjustments that maintain product quality throughout production runs.

Scale-up from pilot to commercial continuous manufacturing presents unique challenges that demand rigorous experimental design and validation. BioNova Global Headquarters has developed standardized protocols for residence time optimization, mixing efficiency analysis, and thermal profiling that help minimize development timelines. Companies transitioning to continuous systems often underestimate the importance of thorough hydrodynamic modeling and computational fluid dynamics simulations before commissioning production equipment.

Training and organizational change management often determine success more than the technology itself. Staff accustomed to batch manufacturing mentality require comprehensive education on continuous process monitoring and troubleshooting. BioNova Global Headquarters emphasizes that successful implementation includes documentation strategies, operator competency frameworks, and robust deviation management procedures specific to continuous operations.

For organizations considering this transition, the long-term benefits clearly justify initial capital expenditure and development effort. Visit bionova.example/continuous-manufacturing to explore our technical resources, case studies, and white papers on best practices for pharmaceutical continuous manufacturing implementation.
clinical_condition_explainer_alzheimers#: Alzheimer disease is a progressive neurological condition that affects memory, thinking, and behavior. It is the most common cause of dementia among older adults, typically developing after age 65, though early-onset forms can appear sooner. Researchers are studying new approaches including gene therapies, amyloid-targeting antibodies, and tau-based interventions. Clinical trials offer access to investigational treatments not yet available to the general public. If you or a family member has been diagnosed with mild-to-moderate Alzheimer disease, a clinical trial may be an option worth exploring with your physician.
clinical_condition_explainer_breast-canc:
Understanding HER2-Positive Breast Cancer

What is HER2-positive breast cancer?

Breast cancer starts when cells in breast tissue grow out of control. HER2-positive breast cancer is a specific type where cancer cells make too much of a protein called HER2. Think of HER2 as a growth signal on the surface of cancer cells. When there's too much of this protein, it tells the cells to multiply faster.

About one in five people with breast cancer have the HER2-positive type. The good news is that doctors have developed treatments designed specifically to target this protein, which can make a real difference in outcomes.

Common symptoms

You might notice one or more of these signs:

A lump or thickening in your breast or underarm area
Changes in breast size or shape
Dimpling or puckering of the skin, like the texture of an orange peel
Redness or scaling around the nipple
Nipple discharge
Pain or tenderness in the breast

Some people have no symptoms at all, which is why screening matters. Any changes in how your breasts look or feel should be discussed with your doctor.

How doctors diagnose HER2-positive breast cancer

Your doctor will start with a physical exam and may order imaging tests like mammograms or ultrasounds to get a closer look. If something suspicious is found, a biopsy is done. This means taking a small tissue sample to examine under a microscope.

A key step is testing that sample for HER2 levels. This test tells your doctor whether the cancer cells are making too much HER2 protein. Getting the right diagnosis is crucial because it determines which treatments will work best for you.

Treatment options

HER2-positive breast cancer is often treated with a combination approach:

Surgery may be recommended to remove the tumor and surrounding tissue.

Chemotherapy uses strong medicines to kill cancer cells throughout your body.

Targeted therapies are medicines designed to attack cancer cells with high HER2 levels while limiting damage to healthy cells. These have changed survival rates for many patients.

Radiation therapy uses focused energy to destroy remaining cancer cells.

Your doctor will create a treatment plan based on your specific situation, including how far the cancer has spread and your overall health.

Clinical trials

Clinical trials are research studies that test new ways to prevent, detect, or treat cancer. They give patients access to cutting-edge treatments before they're widely available.

If you're interested in learning more about clinical trials, including HER2COMBO-201, talk with your healthcare team. They can explain whether a trial might be right for you and what participation involves. Clinical trials help advance care for everyone while potentially offering you additional treatment options.

Moving forward

A diagnosis of HER2-positive breast cancer is serious, but many effective treatments exist. Work closely with your medical team to understand your specific situation and explore all available options. Ask questions, seek support from loved ones or support groups, and remember that advances in treatment continue to improve outcomes for patients like you.

UNDERSTANDING HER2-POSITIVE BREAST CANCER

What Is HER2-Positive Breast Cancer?

HER2-positive breast cancer is a type of breast cancer where cancer cells produce too much of a protein called HER2. Think of HER2 like a growth signal on cancer cells. When there's too much of this protein, it tells the cells to grow and divide faster than normal cells do. About 1 in 5 people with breast cancer have the HER2-positive type.

This doesn't mean your cancer is automatically more serious than other types, but it does grow faster. The good news is that because we know what to look for with HER2, there are treatments designed specifically to target it.

Recognizing the Symptoms

You or a loved one might notice a lump in the breast or armpit, dimpling or puckering of the skin, redness, swelling, or pain in one breast. Some people notice discharge from the nipple or changes in how the breast looks or feels. Not everyone has symptoms early on, which is why screening matters.

How Is It Diagnosed?

When doctors suspect breast cancer, they start with imaging tests like mammograms or ultrasounds to get pictures of the breast. If something looks unusual, they take a small tissue sample (called a biopsy) to examine under a microscope. This is the only way to confirm cancer and to check if it's HER2-positive. Your doctor will run specific tests on the cancer cells to measure HER2 levels.

Treatment Options

Treatment plans are personalized based on your cancer stage, overall health, and preferences. Options may include surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, or targeted therapy.

Targeted therapy is a newer approach that works differently from traditional chemotherapy. These medicines are designed to attack the HER2 protein specifically, which slows cancer growth while potentially causing fewer side effects to healthy cells. Some targeted therapies work by blocking the growth signals, while others attach to the HER2 protein and mark cancer cells for your immune system to destroy.

Many people receive a combination of treatments. You might have surgery first to remove the tumor, followed by chemotherapy or targeted therapy to reduce the risk that cancer returns. Radiation may also be part of the plan.

Clinical Trials: A Path Forward

Clinical trials test new ways to treat cancer. The HER2COMBO-201 trial, for example, is studying new treatment combinations for HER2-positive breast cancer. In a clinical trial, you receive cutting-edge treatment while helping researchers learn what works best for future patients.

Participating is entirely your choice. Clinical trials offer access to new medicines before they're widely available, and your medical team monitors you closely. You can ask questions, discuss concerns, and stop participating at any time.

Moving Forward

A HER2-positive breast cancer diagnosis is serious, but advances in targeted therapy have improved outcomes significantly. Work with your medical team to understand your specific situation and explore all options, including clinical trials if they're right for you. You're not alone in this journey, and support resources are available.
clinical_condition_explainer_breast_canc:
# Understanding HER2-Positive Breast Cancer and Your Treatment Options

## What Is HER2-Positive Breast Cancer?

Breast cancer develops when cells in the breast grow in an uncontrolled way. HER2-positive breast cancer is a specific type where cancer cells have too much of a protein called HER2 (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2) on their surface. Think of HER2 like a growth signal on the cancer cells—these extra signals tell the cells to multiply faster than normal cells do.

About 1 in 5 people with breast cancer have the HER2-positive type. The good news is that doctors have developed medicines specifically designed to target HER2, which has improved treatment outcomes for many patients.

## Recognizing Symptoms

You may notice:

- A lump or thickening in the breast or underarm area
- Changes in the size or shape of the breast
- Nipple discharge or inversion (turning inward)
- Skin changes, dimpling, or redness
- Pain or discomfort in the breast area

These symptoms don't always mean cancer—other conditions can cause them too. But if you notice any changes that last more than a few weeks, talk with your doctor right away.

## How Doctors Diagnose HER2-Positive Breast Cancer

Your doctor will likely start with a physical exam and imaging tests like mammography (breast X-rays) or ultrasound. If an area looks concerning, a biopsy may be recommended—this means taking a small tissue sample to examine under a microscope.

The biopsy is crucial because it allows doctors to confirm cancer and test whether the cancer cells have extra HER2 protein. This information helps your medical team create a treatment plan tailored specifically for you.

## Treatment Options Available

Treatment plans are personalized based on factors like the cancer stage, your overall health, and your preferences. Common approaches include:

**Targeted Therapy:** These medicines work like precision tools, targeting the HER2 protein on cancer cells while sparing healthy cells. Your doctor may recommend one targeted therapy or a combination of medicines to attack the cancer in different ways.

**Chemotherapy:** These medicines kill fast-growing cells throughout the body. They're often used alongside targeted therapy.

**Surgery:** Your surgeon may remove the cancer and surrounding tissue.

**Radiation Therapy:** High-energy beams target cancer cells after surgery.

**Hormone Therapy:** Some breast cancers respond to hormonal medicines.

## Clinical Trials: Another Option to Consider

Clinical trials test new or improved ways to treat cancer. These studies help researchers understand whether new treatment combinations work better than standard treatments.

If you're interested in learning more about available trials—such as HER2COMBO-201—ask your medical team. They can discuss whether a trial might be appropriate for your situation. Participating in a trial means you'll receive careful medical monitoring and may have access to new treatment options before they're widely available.

## Moving Forward

Being diagnosed with HER2-positive breast cancer is challenging, but advances in targeted therapy have transformed this into a condition many people manage successfully. Work closely with your healthcare team, ask questions about your options, and consider reaching out to support groups. You don't have to navigate this alone.

Breast cancer develops when cells in breast tissue grow abnormally and form a tumor. It is one of the most commonly diagnosed cancers worldwide, affecting both women and, rarely, men. Treatment typically involves a combination of surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, and targeted therapies depending on the cancer subtype. HER2-positive breast cancer, for example, responds to specific monoclonal antibody treatments. Clinical trials are testing next-generation targeted therapies and immunotherapies that may offer improved outcomes for patients who have not responded to standard treatments.
clinical_condition_explainer_cardiovascu:
Understanding Heart Disease and What You Can Do About It

Heart disease is one of the most common health conditions affecting people today. If you or a loved one has been diagnosed with heart disease, you're not alone. Understanding what's happening in your body and what options are available can help you feel more confident about managing your health.

What Is Heart Disease?

Your heart is a muscle that pumps blood throughout your body. Blood carries oxygen and nutrients that every part of your body needs to work properly. Heart disease, also called coronary artery disease or ischemic heart disease, happens when the blood vessels that supply your heart become narrow or blocked. This narrowing is usually caused by a buildup of a waxy substance called plaque inside the vessel walls. When blood can't flow freely to your heart muscle, it doesn't get enough oxygen. This can lead to chest pain, shortness of breath, or a heart attack.

Common Risk Factors

Several things can increase your chances of developing heart disease. These include high blood pressure, high cholesterol, smoking, diabetes, being overweight, not exercising enough, and a family history of heart disease. Stress and depression can also play a role. The good news is that many of these risk factors can be managed or improved.

Recognizing the Symptoms

Heart disease symptoms vary from person to person. Some people experience chest pain or pressure, especially during physical activity or stress. Others feel shortness of breath, unusual tiredness, or pain in the arms, neck, or jaw. Women sometimes have different symptoms, like nausea or back pain. If you experience any of these warning signs, contact your doctor right away. With a heart attack, every minute matters.

How Doctors Diagnose Heart Disease

Your doctor will start by asking about your symptoms and family history. They may perform tests like an EKG, which records your heart's electrical activity, or blood tests to check cholesterol and other markers. An imaging test like a stress test or cardiac catheterization might show exactly where blood flow is blocked.

Treatment Options Available

Treatment depends on how severe your condition is. Many people benefit from lifestyle changes like eating a heart-healthy diet, exercising regularly, quitting smoking, and managing stress. Medications can help lower blood pressure, reduce cholesterol, and prevent blood clots. If blockages are severe, procedures like angioplasty or bypass surgery may be recommended.

Clinical Trials: Another Option to Consider

Medical research is constantly working to find better ways to treat heart disease. Clinical trials, like CARDIO-301, test new treatments to see if they work better or have fewer side effects than current options. If your doctor mentions a trial, it means they think you might benefit from learning more about it. Participating in a trial is always your choice, and you can ask as many questions as you need before deciding.

Living with heart disease is challenging, but with proper management and support, many people live full, active lives. Talk with your healthcare team about what's right for you.

Understanding Cardiovascular Disease and Your Heart Health

What Is Cardiovascular Disease?

Your heart is a muscle that pumps blood throughout your body. Cardiovascular disease happens when blood vessels (the tubes that carry blood) become narrow or blocked. The most common type is coronary artery disease, which affects the blood vessels that supply your heart muscle with oxygen-rich blood.

Over time, a fatty substance called plaque can build up inside blood vessels. This buildup makes it harder for blood to flow freely. When your heart doesn't get enough oxygen-rich blood, it can't work as well as it should.

Common Risk Factors

Several things can increase your chance of developing cardiovascular disease. Some you cannot change, like your age or family history. But many risk factors you can influence:

High blood pressure forces your heart to work harder and damages blood vessel walls. High cholesterol allows plaque to build up more easily. Smoking directly damages blood vessels and increases plaque formation. Being overweight or physically inactive puts extra stress on your heart. Diabetes makes blood vessel damage more likely. Chronic stress may also play a role.

Even if you have one or more risk factors, you can take steps to protect your heart health.

What Symptoms Should I Notice?

Some people have no symptoms at all. Others may experience chest pain or discomfort, especially during physical activity or stress. You might feel shortness of breath, unusual tiredness, or pain in your arms, neck, or jaw. These symptoms can be different for different people.

If you suddenly experience severe chest pain, difficulty breathing, or feel faint, call 911 immediately.

How Is It Diagnosed?

Doctors use several tests to check heart health. Blood tests measure cholesterol and other markers. An electrocardiogram records your heart's electrical activity. Stress tests show how your heart responds to exercise. Imaging tests like CT scans or angiograms create pictures of your heart and blood vessels.

Treatment Options

Treatment depends on how severe your disease is. Most people benefit from lifestyle changes like eating heart-healthy foods, exercising regularly, quitting smoking, and managing stress.

Many patients take medications to lower blood pressure, reduce cholesterol, or help their heart work more efficiently. Some medications prevent blood clots from forming.

If blood vessels are severely blocked, doctors may recommend procedures to improve blood flow. Angioplasty opens blocked vessels using a small balloon. Stents are tiny metal tubes placed to keep vessels open. In some cases, bypass surgery reroutes blood around blocked areas.

Clinical Trials and New Options

Researchers are constantly developing new treatments to help people with cardiovascular disease. Clinical trials like CARDIO-301 test whether new approaches work better or have fewer side effects than current treatments.

If you're interested in learning about clinical trials, talk with your doctor. Trials may offer access to new therapies while contributing to medical knowledge that helps others.

Your Next Steps

Managing cardiovascular disease is a partnership between you and your healthcare team. Regular checkups, taking medications as prescribed, and making healthy lifestyle choices all matter. Ask your doctor about your specific risk factors and what you can do to protect your heart.
clinical_condition_explainer_copd#41e03d:
UNDERSTANDING COPD AND YOUR TREATMENT OPTIONS

What Is COPD?

COPD stands for Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease. It's a long-term lung condition that makes it harder for air to move in and out of your lungs. You may have heard it called chronic bronchitis or emphysema—these are types of COPD.

In healthy lungs, air flows freely through tubes called airways. But with COPD, these airways become narrower and less elastic. Your lungs may also produce extra mucus. Over time, this damage gets worse, making it increasingly difficult to breathe.

COPD develops slowly, usually over many years. It's most often caused by smoking, but it can also develop from long-term exposure to air pollution, dust, or other harmful substances. Some people are born with a rare genetic condition that increases their COPD risk. Not everyone with these exposures develops COPD, and not all COPD is caused by smoking.

What Symptoms Should You Watch For?

In the early stages, COPD may feel like a persistent cough you can't shake. As it progresses, you might notice:

A cough that lasts for weeks or months, sometimes bringing up mucus
Shortness of breath, especially during activity
Wheezing or a whistling sound when you breathe
Tightness in your chest
Feeling tired more easily than before

If you notice these symptoms, talk with your doctor. It's important to get checked early.

How Is COPD Diagnosed?

Your doctor will ask about your symptoms and medical history. They'll listen to your lungs with a stethoscope. The main test for COPD is called spirometry—a simple breathing test where you breathe into a small machine that measures how much air your lungs hold and how quickly you can blow it out. This test shows how well your lungs are working.

Your doctor might also order a chest X-ray or CT scan to rule out other conditions.

Treatment Options for COPD

There's no cure for COPD, but treatment can help you breathe better and slow the disease. The main treatments include:

Inhalers: These are handheld devices that deliver medicine directly to your lungs. There are two main types. Rescue inhalers work quickly to open airways during breathing problems. Maintenance inhalers help prevent symptoms by keeping airways open throughout the day.

Lifestyle changes: Quitting smoking is crucial. Regular activity, good nutrition, and avoiding air pollution also help.

Pulmonary rehabilitation: These programs teach breathing techniques and exercises to improve your strength and quality of life.

Medications: Doctors may prescribe pills or other medicines to help manage symptoms.

In more advanced cases, oxygen therapy or surgery might be options.

About Clinical Trials

Researchers are always working to develop better COPD treatments. Clinical trials test new medicines or treatments to see if they're safe and effective. If you're interested in learning more about inhaler therapy trials like BREATHE-101, talk with your doctor. They can discuss whether a trial might be right for you and answer your questions about how trials work.

Managing COPD is a partnership between you and your healthcare team. With proper treatment and support, many people with COPD live full, active lives.
clinical_condition_explainer_copd#d105b6:
# Understanding COPD and How We're Working to Help

If you or a loved one has been told you have COPD, you're not alone. COPD stands for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. It's a long-term condition that makes it harder for your lungs to work the way they should.

## What Happens in COPD

Your lungs are responsible for taking in oxygen and getting rid of carbon dioxide when you breathe. In COPD, the airways in your lungs become narrowed and damaged. This happens over time, usually from smoking or long-term exposure to harmful air. The damage makes it difficult for air to flow in and out of your lungs, leaving you feeling short of breath.

You might have heard other names for this condition, like chronic bronchitis or emphysema. These are actually types of COPD. Chronic bronchitis involves swelling in the airways. Emphysema means the tiny air sacs in your lungs are damaged. Many people have both.

## Recognizing the Symptoms

Common signs of COPD include persistent coughing, especially one that produces mucus. You might feel short of breath during everyday activities like climbing stairs or walking. Some people notice wheezing, a whistling sound when breathing, or tightness in the chest. Fatigue is also common. Symptoms often develop gradually and get worse over time.

## Getting a Diagnosis

If your doctor suspects COPD, they'll likely perform a breathing test called spirometry. This simple test measures how much air your lungs can hold and how quickly you can breathe it out. Your doctor may also take a chest X-ray or do a blood test. Catching COPD early makes managing it easier.

## Treatment Options Available

COPD cannot be cured, but it absolutely can be managed. Treatment focuses on reducing symptoms and slowing the disease's progress.

Inhalers are usually the main treatment. An inhaler is a handheld device that delivers medicine directly into your lungs. There are different types. Some open your airways during a flare-up, while others reduce swelling and mucus regularly. Your doctor will prescribe what works best for you.

Besides inhalers, doctors recommend lifestyle changes like quitting smoking, staying active, and eating well. Pulmonary rehabilitation programs teach breathing techniques and exercises to strengthen your lungs.

## Clinical Trials: New Hope

Researchers are constantly developing new medicines to help people with COPD breathe easier. Clinical trials test whether these new treatments are safe and effective. BioNova Global Headquarters is involved in research like the BREATHE-101 trial, which studies new inhaler therapies.

If your doctor thinks you might be right for a clinical trial, they can discuss the details with you. Trials give you access to cutting-edge treatment while helping researchers help future patients.

## Moving Forward

Living with COPD requires ongoing management, but with the right treatment plan and support, many people maintain good quality of life. Talk regularly with your healthcare team about your symptoms and concerns. They can adjust your treatment as needed.

You have more options than ever before, and new developments are on the horizon.
clinical_condition_explainer_diabetes-t2:
Understanding Type 2 Diabetes

If you've been told you have type 2 diabetes or your doctor has suggested you might develop it, you're not alone. Millions of people live with this condition and manage it successfully every day. Understanding what's happening in your body can help you make informed decisions about your care, including whether a clinical trial might be right for you.

What Is Type 2 Diabetes?

Type 2 diabetes is a condition where your body has trouble managing blood sugar levels. Let me explain what that means. Your body breaks down food into a sugar called glucose, which gives your cells energy. A hormone called insulin acts like a key that helps glucose get into your cells. In type 2 diabetes, two things can happen: either your body doesn't make enough insulin, or your body can't use the insulin it makes effectively. This is sometimes called insulin resistance. When insulin doesn't work properly, glucose builds up in your blood instead of entering your cells, which causes high blood sugar.

This condition usually develops gradually over time, which is why some people call it adult-onset diabetes. It's different from type 1 diabetes, where the immune system stops making insulin entirely.

Recognizing the Symptoms

You might not notice any symptoms at first, since type 2 diabetes often develops slowly. Some people don't know they have it until a routine blood test shows high blood sugar. However, common signs include feeling more thirsty than usual, needing to urinate more often, feeling unusually tired, blurred vision, or cuts and bruises that heal slowly. Some people experience numbness or tingling in their hands or feet.

If you notice any of these signs, talk with your doctor. Catching type 2 diabetes early can help prevent serious health problems down the road.

How Doctors Diagnose It

Your doctor typically uses a simple blood test to diagnose type 2 diabetes. They might order a fasting glucose test (measuring blood sugar after you've fasted overnight), a random glucose test, or an A1C test, which shows your average blood sugar over the past three months. If your test results are higher than normal, your doctor will discuss next steps with you.

Your Current Treatment Options

If you're diagnosed with type 2 diabetes, treatment usually starts with lifestyle changes. Your doctor might recommend eating a balanced diet with plenty of vegetables and whole grains, exercising regularly, and maintaining a healthy weight. These changes really do make a difference for many people.

If lifestyle changes alone aren't enough, your doctor might prescribe medication to help your body use insulin better or to help your pancreas produce more insulin. There are several different types of diabetes medications available, and your doctor will choose based on your individual needs.

Clinical Trials as an Option

Some people are eligible to participate in clinical trials like DIABPREV-201, which test new approaches to preventing or treating type 2 diabetes. Trials can offer access to new treatments before they're widely available, plus close monitoring by medical professionals. If you're interested in learning whether a trial might be appropriate for you, ask your doctor about your options. They can help you understand what's involved and whether it's a good fit for your situation.

Understanding Type 2 Diabetes

What is Type 2 Diabetes?

Type 2 diabetes is a condition where your body has trouble managing blood sugar levels. Your blood sugar, also called glucose, is the fuel your body uses for energy. When you eat, food breaks down into glucose, which enters your bloodstream. Normally, your pancreas makes a hormone called insulin that acts like a key, helping glucose move from your blood into your cells where it gets used for energy.

In type 2 diabetes, something goes wrong with this process. Your cells become resistant to insulin, meaning the key doesn't work as well as it should. Even though your pancreas tries to make more insulin to help glucose get into your cells, it's not enough. This causes glucose to build up in your bloodstream instead of being used by your cells.

Type 2 diabetes develops slowly over time, which is why some people call it adult-onset diabetes, though it can happen at any age. It's the most common type of diabetes.

Symptoms You Might Notice

Many people with type 2 diabetes don't realize they have it at first because symptoms develop gradually. You might notice:

Increased thirst and more frequent urination, especially at night. Feeling unusually tired or fatigued. Blurred vision. Cuts or sores that heal slowly. Tingling or numbness in your hands or feet.

Some people have no symptoms at all and only discover they have diabetes during a routine doctor's visit.

How Doctors Diagnose It

Your doctor can diagnose type 2 diabetes through blood tests. The most common tests measure your fasting blood sugar (after not eating for several hours) or use an A1C test, which shows your average blood sugar over the past three months. Your doctor might also do a glucose tolerance test, where you drink a sugary liquid and they check how your body handles it.

Current Treatment Options

Managing type 2 diabetes usually involves lifestyle changes combined with medication:

Lifestyle changes are the foundation: eating healthier foods, staying active, losing weight if needed, and managing stress. These changes can sometimes delay or prevent the need for medication.

Medications help your body use insulin better or help your pancreas make more insulin. There are many different types, and doctors choose based on your individual needs.

Monitoring your blood sugar at home helps you and your doctor see how well your treatment is working.

About Clinical Trials

If you're considering a clinical trial like DIABPREV-201, it's a way to potentially access new treatment options while contributing to medical research. Clinical trials test whether new approaches work safely and effectively. BioNova Global Headquarters conducts these studies to develop better ways to help people manage type 2 diabetes.

Before joining any trial, ask your doctor questions about what's involved, potential benefits and risks, and whether it's right for you. Your participation is always voluntary, and you can ask to withdraw at any time.

Type 2 diabetes is manageable with the right combination of treatment and lifestyle support. Talk with your healthcare team about all your options.
clinical_condition_explainer_diabetes_t2:
Understanding Type 2 Diabetes

Type 2 diabetes is a condition where your body has trouble managing blood sugar levels. Blood sugar, also called glucose, is fuel that comes from the food you eat. Normally, your pancreas makes a hormone called insulin that acts like a key, helping glucose enter your cells so they can use it for energy. In type 2 diabetes, your body either doesn't make enough insulin or doesn't use it well. This is sometimes called insulin resistance. When insulin doesn't work properly, glucose builds up in your bloodstream instead of entering your cells. Over time, high blood sugar can damage your heart, kidneys, eyes, and nerves.

Type 2 diabetes develops slowly, often over many years. It's the most common type of diabetes, affecting millions of adults. It's sometimes called adult-onset diabetes, though it can happen at any age.

Common Symptoms

Many people don't notice symptoms at first. That's why some people don't know they have it until a blood test shows high blood sugar. When symptoms do appear, they may include:

Increased thirst and frequent urination. You might feel thirsty more often and need to urinate more, especially at night. Unusual fatigue. You may feel tired even when you've rested well. Blurred vision. Your eyesight might become unclear. Cuts or sores that heal slowly. Infections may also happen more often or take longer to heal.

How It's Diagnosed

Your doctor can find type 2 diabetes through simple blood tests. The most common test measures your fasting blood sugar, which is your glucose level after not eating for eight hours. Another test called an A1C shows your average blood sugar over the past two to three months. Your doctor might also do an oral glucose tolerance test, where you drink a sugary liquid and have your blood tested afterward.

Treatment Options

Type 2 diabetes is manageable. Treatment usually starts with lifestyle changes like eating healthier foods, moving your body more, and losing weight if needed. These changes can sometimes bring blood sugar back to normal levels.

If lifestyle changes alone aren't enough, your doctor may prescribe medications. Several types exist, each working differently. Some help your pancreas make more insulin. Others help your body use insulin better. Some slow digestion so glucose enters your bloodstream more slowly. Newer medications can help your kidneys and heart too.

Some people eventually need insulin therapy, where insulin is injected under the skin. This is not a sign of failure. It's simply another tool to manage your condition.

Why Clinical Trials Matter

If you're considering a clinical trial like DIABPREV-201, you're exploring new approaches that might help manage type 2 diabetes better. Research helps doctors find treatments that work for more people. Trials test whether new medications are safe and effective before they become widely available.

If you have type 2 diabetes, talk with your doctor about your options. Whether you choose standard treatment, clinical trials, or both, the goal is the same: keeping your blood sugar in a healthy range and preventing complications. You have choices, and your doctor can help you decide what's right for you.

Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a chronic metabolic condition in which the body does not use insulin effectively, leading to elevated blood sugar levels. It affects hundreds of millions of people worldwide and is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, kidney damage, and nerve damage. Management typically includes lifestyle modifications, oral medications such as metformin, and sometimes injectable therapies. Researchers are investigating novel drug classes including dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonists that may offer improved blood sugar control with additional metabolic benefits.
clinical_condition_explainer_hypertensio:
Understanding High Blood Pressure

What is High Blood Pressure?

High blood pressure, also called hypertension, happens when the force of blood pushing against your artery walls is too strong. Think of your arteries like garden hoses. Normal blood pressure is like water flowing through a hose at a steady, comfortable pressure. High blood pressure is like turning the faucet up too high — the extra force can damage the hose over time.

Your doctor measures blood pressure in two numbers. The first number (systolic pressure) shows the force when your heart beats. The second number (diastolic pressure) shows the force when your heart rests between beats. High blood pressure is usually defined as 130/80 or higher, though your doctor can explain what numbers mean for you specifically.

Why Does This Matter?

When blood pressure stays high over months and years, it can damage your blood vessels and organs — especially your heart, kidneys, and brain. This damage increases your risk for serious conditions like heart attacks and strokes. That's why doctors call high blood pressure a risk factor for cardiovascular disease (disease affecting your heart and blood vessels). Catching and treating high blood pressure early can help prevent these serious health problems.

What Symptoms Might You Notice?

Here's something important to know: most people with high blood pressure don't feel anything wrong. You might feel fine even when your blood pressure is dangerously high. This is why some doctors call it the "silent killer." Some people do experience headaches, shortness of breath, or nosebleeds, but these aren't reliable warning signs.

The only way to know if you have high blood pressure is to have it checked. This is why regular checkups matter, especially as you get older or if high blood pressure runs in your family.

How is High Blood Pressure Diagnosed?

Your doctor uses a simple blood pressure monitor — usually a cuff around your arm — to measure your pressure. You might get checked once at an appointment, or your doctor might ask you to check it several times over weeks to see if it's consistently high. Some people use home monitors to track their pressure between doctor visits.

What Treatment Options Exist?

Treatment usually starts with lifestyle changes. These might include eating less salt, exercising regularly, managing stress, limiting alcohol, and maintaining a healthy weight. Many people find these changes help.

If lifestyle changes alone don't lower your blood pressure enough, your doctor might recommend medication. Several different types of blood pressure medicines work in different ways to help relax blood vessels or reduce the amount of fluid in your body.

Researchers are also working on new ways to treat high blood pressure. BioNova Global Headquarters is involved in clinical research, including a study called CARDIO-301, that tests new treatment approaches. If you're interested in learning whether a clinical trial might be right for you, talk with your doctor.

The key is working closely with your healthcare team to manage your blood pressure and reduce your risk for serious heart and blood vessel problems.

# Understanding High Blood Pressure and Heart Health

## What Is High Blood Pressure?

High blood pressure, also called hypertension, happens when the force of blood pushing against your artery walls is too strong over a long period of time. Think of it like water pressure in a garden hose—when the pressure gets too high, it can damage the hose. The same thing happens to your arteries.

Blood pressure is measured with two numbers. The top number shows the pressure when your heart beats. The bottom number shows the pressure when your heart rests between beats. A healthy blood pressure is below 120/80. High blood pressure is 130/80 or higher.

## Why Should You Care?

High blood pressure often has no symptoms—many people don't even know they have it. That's why doctors call it the "silent killer." But over time, this extra pressure can damage your heart, kidneys, brain, and blood vessels. This increases your risk of serious health problems like heart attacks and strokes.

The longer you have high blood pressure without treatment, the more damage it can cause. That's why catching it early and managing it matters.

## Signs to Watch For

Most people with high blood pressure feel completely normal. You can't feel your blood pressure rising. However, some people may notice:

- Headaches
- Shortness of breath
- Nosebleeds
- Chest discomfort

If you have these symptoms, don't wait—see a doctor right away.

## How Is It Diagnosed?

Your doctor checks your blood pressure with a simple tool called a blood pressure cuff. It wraps around your arm and takes a quick reading. Because blood pressure changes throughout the day, your doctor may check it several times or ask you to monitor it at home.

Sometimes doctors use other tests to understand how high blood pressure is affecting your health. These might include blood tests, urine tests, or heart tests.

## Treatment Options

There are many ways to manage high blood pressure:

**Lifestyle changes** often help first. These include eating less salt, exercising regularly, losing weight if needed, limiting alcohol, and managing stress.

**Medicines** can help lower blood pressure if lifestyle changes aren't enough. Many different types exist, and your doctor will choose what works best for you.

**Monitoring** at home helps you and your doctor track how well your treatment is working.

## Clinical Research

Scientists are always working to find better ways to help people with high blood pressure. Clinical trials like CARDIO-301 test new treatments and approaches. If you're interested in joining a research study, talk with your doctor about whether it might be right for you.

## Next Steps

If you think you might have high blood pressure, schedule a visit with your doctor. Regular checkups help catch problems early. If you already know you have high blood pressure, take your medicines as prescribed and keep your appointments.

For more information about managing your health, speak with your healthcare team. They know your situation best and can give you personalized guidance.

Essential hypertension, commonly known as high blood pressure, is a chronic condition in which the force of blood against artery walls is consistently elevated. It is a major risk factor for heart disease, stroke, and kidney disease. While many effective treatments exist, including ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and calcium channel blockers, researchers continue to develop therapies that may offer better blood pressure control with fewer side effects. Clinical trials investigating novel antihypertensive agents and combination therapies are ongoing at research centers worldwide.
clinical_condition_explainer_lung-cancer:
UNDERSTANDING NON-SMALL CELL LUNG CANCER AND YOUR TREATMENT OPTIONS

What Is Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer?

Non-small cell lung cancer, or NSCLC, is a type of lung cancer that starts in the cells of your lungs. Your lungs are organs that help you breathe by bringing oxygen into your body. NSCLC accounts for about 85 percent of all lung cancer cases. Like other cancers, NSCLC happens when cells in your lung grow in an uncontrolled way and can spread to other parts of your body.

Common Symptoms

Many people don't notice symptoms in the early stages of NSCLC. When symptoms do appear, they may include:

A cough that lasts more than three weeks
Chest pain or discomfort
Shortness of breath
Hoarseness in your voice
Coughing up blood
Fatigue or weakness
Loss of appetite or unexplained weight loss

If you're experiencing any of these symptoms, talk with your doctor. Having symptoms doesn't necessarily mean you have cancer, but it's important to get checked.

How Is NSCLC Diagnosed?

Your doctor will use several tools to diagnose and understand your cancer:

Imaging tests like chest X-rays or CT scans take pictures of your lungs to look for abnormal areas.

A biopsy involves taking a small tissue sample from your lung so doctors can examine the cells under a microscope.

Blood tests help your doctor understand more about your cancer and how your body is responding.

Your doctor may also perform genetic testing on your cancer cells. This testing looks for specific changes in the cancer's DNA. Understanding these changes is important because it helps determine which treatments might work best for you.

Treatment Options: Immunotherapy and Targeted Therapy

Treatment for NSCLC depends on many factors, including the stage of your cancer, your overall health, and the genetic makeup of your cancer cells.

Immunotherapy helps your own immune system fight cancer. Your immune system normally protects your body from harmful invaders, but cancer cells can hide from it. Immunotherapy drugs help your immune system recognize and attack cancer cells. These treatments can help some people live longer and improve their quality of life.

Targeted therapy works differently. These drugs are designed to attack specific changes or mutations found in cancer cells. If genetic testing shows your cancer has certain mutations, targeted therapy may be an option. These drugs work like a key fitting into a lock—they target the specific weaknesses in your cancer cells while sparing many healthy cells.

Clinical Trials: An Option to Consider

Clinical trials are research studies that test new ways to treat cancer. Companies like BioNova are conducting trials to learn whether new immunotherapy and targeted therapy combinations work better than current treatments.

Some trials currently enrolling patients include ONCORA-301 and ONCORA-101. Being part of a clinical trial means you receive careful medical monitoring and may have access to new treatments. Trials also help advance treatment options for future patients.

Your Next Steps

If you've been diagnosed with NSCLC, talk with your doctor about all available treatment options, including clinical trials. Your medical team can help you understand which approaches might be right for your specific situation. Remember, you're not alone in this journey.

Understanding Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer and Your Treatment Options

What is Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer?

Non-small cell lung cancer, or NSCLC, is a type of lung cancer that starts in the cells lining the airways or air sacs of the lungs. About 85 out of every 100 people diagnosed with lung cancer have NSCLC. This cancer develops when healthy lung cells begin to grow in an uncontrolled way. The cancer can spread to other parts of the body if not treated.

NSCLC develops relatively quickly, which is why doctors recommend prompt medical attention once a diagnosis is confirmed.

Common Symptoms to Watch For

You or a loved one may have experienced one or more of these symptoms before diagnosis:

A cough that lasts more than three weeks
Chest pain or discomfort
Shortness of breath or wheezing
Coughing up blood
Hoarseness in your voice
Fatigue or weakness
Unexplained weight loss

These symptoms can be caused by other conditions too, so it is important to have them evaluated by a healthcare provider.

How is NSCLC Diagnosed?

Doctors use several tests to diagnose and understand your specific cancer:

Imaging tests like CT scans or X-rays to see the lungs
Biopsies, where a small tissue sample is removed and examined under a microscope
Blood tests to check overall health
Additional tests to understand specific features of your cancer cells

Understanding these features is especially important because it helps your doctor recommend the most effective treatment approach for you.

Treatment Options: Immunotherapy and Targeted Therapy

Two major treatment approaches have transformed NSCLC care in recent years:

Immunotherapy works by teaching your body's own immune system to recognize and fight cancer cells. Your immune system normally protects you from harmful invaders, but cancer cells can hide from it. Immunotherapy removes that disguise, helping your body's defenses attack the cancer.

Targeted therapy uses drugs designed to attack specific parts of cancer cells that make them grow. If your cancer has certain genetic changes or mutations, targeted therapy drugs can be very effective because they focus on those specific weaknesses. Not all patients have these mutations, which is why testing is important.

Clinical Trials: An Additional Option

Clinical trials test new treatment combinations and approaches. Some patients with NSCLC may be eligible for trials like ONCORA-301 and ONCORA-101, which evaluate immunotherapy and targeted therapy approaches. Participating in a trial offers potential access to newer treatments while helping doctors learn what works best for future patients.

Your healthcare team can discuss whether a clinical trial might be right for you based on your specific diagnosis and health situation.

Moving Forward

A diagnosis of NSCLC can feel overwhelming, but there are multiple treatment pathways available. Your medical team will help you understand your options and create a plan tailored to your cancer's characteristics and your personal goals.

If you have questions about your diagnosis or treatment choices, speak openly with your healthcare provider. They are there to support you through this journey.
clinical_condition_explainer_lung_cancer: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) is the most common type of lung cancer, accounting for approximately 85% of all lung cancer diagnoses. Treatment options depend on the stage at diagnosis and may include surgery, radiation therapy, chemotherapy, targeted therapy, and immunotherapy. Recent advances in immunotherapy and bispecific antibodies have shown promise for patients with advanced disease. Clinical trials are testing new treatment combinations and novel agents that may extend survival and improve quality of life for patients with NSCLC.

Understanding Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer and Your Treatment Options

What is Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer?

Non-small cell lung cancer, or NSCLC, is a type of lung cancer that starts in the cells lining your airways and lungs. It's called "non-small cell" because the cancer cells look larger under a microscope compared to another type of lung cancer. NSCLC is the most common form of lung cancer, accounting for about 8 out of 10 lung cancer cases.

Your lungs are responsible for taking in oxygen when you breathe and releasing carbon dioxide when you exhale. When cancer develops in your lungs, these healthy cells begin to grow in an abnormal, uncontrolled way.

Common Symptoms

You may notice one or more of these signs:

A cough that lasts more than three weeks
Chest pain or discomfort, especially when breathing or coughing
Shortness of breath
Hoarseness or changes in your voice
Coughing up blood
Fatigue or weakness
Recurring lung infections like bronchitis or pneumonia

It's important to know that having these symptoms doesn't mean you definitely have lung cancer. Only your doctor can make that diagnosis. If you've been experiencing any of these symptoms, talk with your healthcare provider.

How NSCLC is Diagnosed

Your doctor will start by asking about your symptoms and medical history. You may undergo several tests:

Imaging tests like chest X-rays or CT scans create detailed pictures of your lungs
A biopsy involves taking a small sample of lung tissue to examine under a microscope
Blood tests provide information about your overall health
Additional tests may help identify specific features of your cancer

These tests help your doctor understand the extent of your cancer and plan the best approach for you.

Understanding Your Treatment Options

Once you're diagnosed, your medical team will discuss treatment possibilities. Two newer approaches show promise for NSCLC:

Immunotherapy uses medicines that help your own immune system recognize and fight cancer cells. Your immune system naturally protects you from harmful invaders, but cancer cells sometimes hide from it. Immunotherapy teaches your immune system to spot and attack these cancer cells more effectively.

Targeted therapy works differently. Your doctor may test your cancer cells for specific genetic changes or mutations—think of these as "mistakes" in the cancer cell's instructions. Targeted therapies are designed to attack cancer cells with these specific mutations while doing less harm to healthy cells. This approach is like using a precision tool rather than a broader approach.

Your doctor will discuss which option might be appropriate based on your specific cancer's characteristics, your overall health, and other factors.

Clinical Trials: Another Path Forward

BioNova Global Headquarters is conducting research studies, including trials like ONCORA-301 and ONCORA-101, that explore new ways to treat NSCLC using immunotherapy and targeted therapy approaches. These trials give eligible patients access to newer treatments while contributing to medical knowledge that may help future patients.

Talking with your healthcare team about whether a clinical trial might be right for you is an important conversation to have. They can explain the potential benefits and risks based on your individual situation.

You're not alone in this journey. Your medical team is here to support you through diagnosis, treatment decisions, and beyond.
clinical_consent_summary_cardio-outcomes:
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW ABOUT THE CARDIO-301 STUDY

This summary explains the BioNova CARDIO-301 research study in plain language. It is not a legal document. Before you join any study, you will receive and sign a full consent form. Please read both documents carefully and ask questions.

1. PURPOSE OF THIS STUDY

The CARDIO-301 study tests whether a new medicine called BNV-CRD01 can help people with heart disease. The study is looking at whether this drug can reduce the risk of heart problems. About 600 people will take part in this study, sponsored by BioNova Therapeutics.

2. WHO CAN JOIN THIS STUDY

You may be able to join if you:
- Are between 40 and 80 years old
- Have coronary artery disease (narrowed blood vessels in the heart) or a similar heart condition
- Have blood pressure above 140 mmHg, even while taking blood pressure medicine
- Have high cholesterol levels (LDL-C above 70 mg/dL)
- Are healthy enough to participate (measured by a standard health assessment)

You cannot join if you:
- Have severe heart failure (a condition where the heart cannot pump blood well)
- Have had a recent stroke
- Have very low kidney function (eGFR below 30 mL/min)
- Are scheduled for a heart procedure in the next 3 months
- Have uncontrolled atrial fibrillation (an irregular heartbeat)

3. WHAT HAPPENS IF YOU JOIN

If you decide to participate, you will:
- Visit the study clinic multiple times over the course of the trial
- Be randomly assigned to receive one of three treatments: the 10-milligram dose of BNV-CRD01, the 25-milligram dose of BNV-CRD01, or a placebo (a pill with no active medicine)
- Have an equal chance of receiving any of these treatments
- Take your assigned treatment by mouth, usually once daily
- Have blood tests done regularly to check your health and how the medicine works
- Complete health questionnaires about how you feel
- Report any side effects or health changes to the study team

4. DURATION

The study will last several months to a few years, depending on how long researchers need to gather information. The study team will tell you the expected timeline before you join.

5. POSSIBLE RISKS

Like all medicines, BNV-CRD01 may cause side effects. We do not yet know all possible side effects because this is still being tested. Possible risks include:
- Nausea, headache, or dizziness
- Changes in blood pressure or heart rate
- Lab test abnormalities
- Unknown side effects that have not yet been identified

In rare cases, serious side effects can occur. The study team will monitor you closely and can stop your participation if needed to keep you safe.

6. POSSIBLE BENEFITS

You may benefit from taking part in this study:
- You will receive careful medical attention and regular monitoring
- You may receive a medicine that could help prevent heart problems
- You will contribute to medical research that could help many people in the future

However, there is no guarantee that BNV-CRD01 will help you personally. You might receive the placebo instead of the active medicine.

7. ALTERNATIVES

If you choose not to join this study, you can continue your regular heart care with your doctor. Your doctor can discuss other available treatments for your condition.

8. YOUR PRIVACY AND CONFIDENTIALITY

Your health information will be kept private and protected by law. Only study staff and regulatory agencies will see your information. Your name will not be used in any study results. A code number will identify you instead.

9. VOLUNTARY PARTICIPATION

Joining this study is completely voluntary. You can say no, and it will not affect your regular medical care. If you join and then change your mind, you can leave at any time without penalty or loss of benefits.

10. QUESTIONS AND CONTACT

Ask the study team any questions before you decide. You deserve to understand what you are agreeing to.

CARDIO-301 Trial: What You Need to Know Before You Join

This summary explains what it means to participate in the CARDIO-301 research study. This is not a legal document. Before you decide to join, you will receive and review the full consent form with your study doctor.

1. PURPOSE OF THIS STUDY

The CARDIO-301 study tests whether a new medicine called BNV-CRD01 can help people with heart disease. Specifically, researchers want to see if this medicine can lower heart attack risk and improve heart health in adults who have narrowing of the arteries that supply blood to the heart (coronary artery disease). BioNova Therapeutics is sponsoring this research.

2. WHO CAN JOIN THIS STUDY

You may be eligible if you are between 40 and 80 years old and have:

- Documented coronary artery disease (narrowing of heart arteries) or an equivalent risk condition
- High blood pressure above 140 mmHg, even with current blood pressure medications
- High cholesterol (LDL-C above 70 mg/dL), a type of cholesterol that can build up in arteries

You cannot join if you have:

- Severe heart failure (NYHA Class IV, meaning your heart is severely weakened)
- A recent stroke
- Very poor kidney function (eGFR below 30 mL/min—eGFR is a measure of how well your kidneys work)
- A planned heart procedure within the next 3 months
- Uncontrolled atrial fibrillation (a serious irregular heartbeat that cannot be managed with current medicines)

Your doctor will discuss whether this study is right for you.

3. WHAT HAPPENS IN THIS STUDY

If you join, you will be assigned randomly to one of three groups—like a flip of a coin:

- Group 1: 10 mg of BNV-CRD01
- Group 2: 25 mg of BNV-CRD01
- Group 3: Placebo (a pill with no active medicine)

Neither you nor your doctor will know which group you are in. This helps keep results fair and honest.

The study will enroll about 600 people total. Your study doctor will explain the exact duration, visit schedule, and tests you will need. You will likely have blood tests, heart monitoring, and check-in visits over several months.

4. POSSIBLE RISKS

Like all medicines, BNV-CRD01 may cause side effects. Risks may include:

- Mild side effects such as nausea, headache, or dizziness
- Serious side effects are possible but have not been clearly identified yet because this is an early-stage study

You could also have risks from study procedures, such as discomfort from blood draws or time away from work. Your study doctor will discuss all known risks in detail before you sign the consent form.

5. POSSIBLE BENEFITS

You may benefit from closer monitoring of your heart health and more frequent doctor visits. However, we cannot promise that BNV-CRD01 will help you personally. The real benefit of this study is to help future patients by providing information about whether this medicine works.

6. ALTERNATIVES

You can continue your current heart medications and care without joining this study. Talk with your regular doctor about other treatment options available to you.

7. PRIVACY AND CONFIDENTIALITY

Your medical information will be protected by law and kept confidential. Only study staff and regulatory agencies will have access to your information. Your identity will not be shared publicly.

8. VOLUNTARY PARTICIPATION

Joining this study is completely voluntary. You can refuse to join, or you can quit at any time without penalty or loss of benefits. Your regular medical care will not be affected by your decision.

If you have questions, ask your study doctor anytime.
clinical_consent_summary_cardio_outcomes:
CARDIO-301 Trial: What You Need to Know About Joining

This summary explains what you would be agreeing to if you decide to participate in the CARDIO-301 research study. Please read this carefully and discuss it with your doctor and loved ones before making your decision.

1. What Is This Study About?

The CARDIO-301 study is testing a new medicine called BNV-CRD01 to see if it can help people with heart disease. Heart disease happens when blood vessels that supply your heart become narrowed or blocked, making it harder for your heart to pump blood. We want to learn if this medicine can reduce your risk of future heart problems and help you live a healthier life. The company BioNova Therapeutics is sponsoring this research.

2. Who Can Join?

You may be eligible if you are:
- Between 40 and 80 years old
- Have been diagnosed with coronary artery disease (when the vessels supplying your heart are narrowed) or have serious risk factors for it
- Have a blood pressure reading of 140 or higher even while taking blood pressure medicine
- Have an LDL cholesterol level above 70 mg/dL (this is "bad" cholesterol that can build up in your arteries)
- In generally stable health, able to walk and care for yourself with minimal help

You cannot join if you have advanced heart failure, recently had a stroke, have serious kidney problems, or have irregular heart rhythms that are not controlled.

3. What Will You Do in This Study?

The study involves about 600 people total. You would be randomly assigned, like a coin flip, to receive one of three treatments:
- The lower dose of BNV-CRD01 (10 milligrams)
- The higher dose of BNV-CRD01 (25 milligrams)
- A placebo (a pill with no active medicine)

Neither you nor your doctor will know which treatment you receive. This helps make sure the results are fair and unbiased.

4. What Procedures Will You Undergo?

You will have regular clinic visits to:
- Check your blood pressure and heart rate
- Answer questions about how you are feeling
- Have blood drawn to measure cholesterol and other heart-related markers
- Possibly have heart tests such as an electrocardiogram (a recording of your heart's electrical activity)

These visits will happen more frequently at the start of the study and then less often. The entire study will last several months to a year, though the exact duration will be explained in your full consent form.

5. What Are the Potential Risks?

Like all medicines, BNV-CRD01 could cause side effects. Common side effects in early testing included mild headache, nausea, and upset stomach. Some people experienced dizziness or slight changes in blood pressure. Serious side effects are possible but have been rare so far. There is also a small risk of complications from blood tests or other procedures.

6. What Are the Potential Benefits?

You may benefit if BNV-CRD01 helps lower your cholesterol, reduce your blood pressure, or decrease your risk of a heart attack or stroke. However, there is no guarantee the medicine will help you personally. Even if you receive a placebo, you will get closer monitoring of your heart health.

7. What Other Choices Do You Have?

You can continue with your current heart medicines and care without joining this study. Your doctor can discuss other treatment options available to you.

8. How Will Your Privacy Be Protected?

Your medical information will be kept confidential and stored securely. It will be identified by a study number rather than your name. Only the research team will have access to connect your information to you.

9. Is Participation Voluntary?

Yes. You can join or not join without any penalty. If you join, you can leave at any time without giving a reason. Leaving the study will not affect your regular medical care.

If you have questions, ask the research team before you decide.
clinical_consent_summary_copd-inhaler#9f:
BREATHE-101 Trial: What You Need to Know Before You Join

This summary explains the BREATHE-101 study in plain language. It tells you what the study is about, what will happen if you join, and what risks and benefits you might experience. This is not a legal document. Before you sign anything, you will receive a full consent form to read and keep.

1. What Is This Study About?

BREATHE-101 is a Phase 1 clinical trial. This is an early-stage study to test whether a new medicine called BNV-CPD01 is safe and works in people with COPD. COPD stands for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, a long-term lung condition that makes it hard to breathe. BioNova Therapeutics is sponsoring this research.

The study will enroll about 80 people. Some participants will receive the study drug at a lower dose, some at a higher dose, and some will receive a placebo (a fake medicine that looks like the real drug but has no active ingredient). Neither you nor the study team will know which treatment you receive at first.

2. Who Can Join?

You may be able to join if you:

• Are between 40 and 80 years old
• Have been diagnosed with COPD
• Are able to do light activity (the study uses a scale called ECOG to measure this)
• Have lung function test results (called FEV1) between 30% and 70% of what is expected for your age and size
• Have had at least one flare-up, or exacerbation, of your COPD in the past 12 months
• Are a current or former smoker with at least 10 pack-years of smoking history (a pack-year means smoking one pack per day for one year)

You cannot join if you:

• Have active asthma along with COPD
• Have been diagnosed with lung cancer
• Use supplemental oxygen at more than 4 liters per minute
• Have had a respiratory infection in the past 4 weeks
• Have had a lung transplant or lung volume reduction surgery

3. What Will Happen During the Study?

If you join, you will visit the study clinic multiple times over several months. At each visit, you will have tests such as lung function tests, blood tests, and chest imaging. You will answer questions about your symptoms and how you are feeling. The study team will watch you closely to make sure the medicine is safe.

You will take the study medicine or placebo as directed. It is very important that you take it exactly as instructed and keep all your appointments.

4. How Long Does It Last?

The study lasts several months from the time you join until the final visit. The exact length will be explained to you in detail.

5. What Are the Risks?

Like all medicines, BNV-CPD01 may cause side effects. Because this is a Phase 1 study, we are still learning about possible side effects. They could range from mild to serious. Common side effects in early testing might include headache, nausea, or cough. More serious side effects are possible but uncommon. The study team will monitor you carefully and will tell you about any new risks as they learn more.

6. What Are the Benefits?

There is no guarantee that you will benefit personally from this study. Some people may notice that their symptoms improve, but others may not see any change. The main benefit is helping researchers learn whether this new medicine could help people with COPD in the future.

7. What Are My Other Choices?

You do not have to join this study. You can continue taking your current COPD medicines. Your doctor can discuss other treatment options with you.

8. How Will My Privacy Be Protected?

Your medical information will be kept confidential. Your name will not appear in any study reports. Your data will be stored securely and shared only with people who need it for the study.

9. Can I Leave the Study?

Yes. You can stop participating at any time for any reason. You do not have to explain why. Stopping will not affect your regular medical care.

For questions, contact BioNova Global Headquarters using the information provided in your full consent form.
clinical_consent_summary_copd-inhaler#c2:
BREATHE-101 Trial: What You Need to Know Before You Join

This summary explains what it means to participate in the BREATHE-101 research study. Please read this carefully before you decide whether to join. This is not a legal document—it's meant to help you understand the study in plain language. If you have questions, ask the study team before you agree to participate.

1. What is this study about?

The BREATHE-101 study tests a new medicine called BNV-CPD01 in people with COPD. COPD stands for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, a long-term lung condition that makes breathing difficult. The study is sponsored by BioNova Therapeutics and led by BioNova Global Headquarters.

Researchers want to learn if BNV-CPD01 is safe and tolerated (meaning your body can handle it without serious problems) in people with moderate COPD. This is an early-stage study called a Phase 1 trial, which means the medicine has not been tested in many people yet.

2. Who can join?

You may be eligible if you:
- Are between 40 and 80 years old
- Have COPD
- Have a lung function level (measured by a test called FEV1) between 30% and 70% of what is normal for your age and size
- Have had at least one COPD flare-up (called an exacerbation) in the past 12 months
- Currently smoke or used to smoke, with a history of at least 10 pack-years (for example, smoking one pack per day for 10 years)

You cannot join if you:
- Have active asthma or lung cancer
- Use supplemental oxygen (bottled air) at doses higher than 4 liters per minute
- Have had a respiratory infection within the past 4 weeks
- Have had a lung transplant or lung volume reduction surgery in the past

3. What will happen if you join?

About 80 people will participate in this study. You will be randomly assigned (like a coin flip) to one of three groups:
- Low-dose BNV-CPD01
- High-dose BNV-CPD01
- Placebo (a treatment with no active medicine)

You will have an equal chance of being in any group. You and the study team will not know which group you're in during the study.

The study will include clinic visits where researchers will:
- Check your vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, temperature)
- Perform lung function tests
- Take blood and urine samples
- Ask about any symptoms or health changes
- Give you the study medicine or placebo

The exact number of visits and duration of the study will be explained in detail during your screening appointment.

4. What are the possible risks?

Because this is a new medicine being tested for the first time in people, risks are not fully known. Possible side effects may include:
- Nausea or upset stomach
- Headache
- Dizziness
- Cough or throat irritation
- Allergic reactions

More serious side effects are possible but uncommon in Phase 1 studies. The study team will monitor you closely and stop your participation if serious problems occur.

5. What are the possible benefits?

There is no guarantee you will benefit from this study. You may experience improved lung function or fewer breathing problems, but this cannot be promised. The main benefit is helping researchers learn whether this new medicine is safe, which may help future patients.

6. What are other options?

You do not have to join this study. You can continue with your current COPD treatments. Talk with your doctor about all your treatment options.

7. How will your information be protected?

Your personal health information will be kept confidential and stored securely. Your name will not appear in any reports. Only the study team and regulatory agencies will see your full medical records. Information may be shared with BioNova Therapeutics.

8. Voluntary participation

Joining this study is completely voluntary. You can stop participating at any time without penalty or loss of any benefits you are entitled to. Leaving the study will not affect your regular medical care.

If you have questions, ask the study team anytime.
clinical_consent_summary_copd_inhaler#c2:
BREATHE-101 Study: What You Need to Know Before You Join

This summary explains what happens if you decide to take part in the BREATHE-101 research study. Please read this carefully. Your doctor or the study team can answer any questions you have.

1. What Is This Study About?

BREATHE-101 is a research study testing a new medicine called BNV-CPD01. This medicine is being developed to help people with COPD (chronic obstructive pulmonary disease), a long-term lung condition that makes it hard to breathe. The study will see if BNV-CPD01 is safe and how it affects your symptoms and lung function. About 80 people will participate in this study. BioNova Therapeutics is sponsoring the research.

2. Who Can Join?

You may be eligible to join if you:

- Are between 40 and 80 years old
- Have been diagnosed with COPD
- Are a current or former smoker with at least a 10 pack-year history (for example, smoking one pack per day for 10 years)
- Have experienced at least one COPD flare-up (exacerbation) in the past 12 months
- Have lung function between 30% and 70% of what is expected for your age and size
- Are well enough to participate, with limited activity restrictions

You cannot join if you:
