- Have had a respiratory infection in the past 4 weeks
- Have had a lung transplant or lung volume reduction surgery in the past

3. What Will Happen During the Study?

You will be assigned randomly to one of three groups. Random assignment means the computer decides which group you join—not you or your doctor. The three groups are:

- Low-dose BNV-CPD01
- High-dose BNV-CPD01
- Placebo (a fake medicine with no active ingredient)

You will not know which group you are in. This helps researchers get fair results.

During the study, you will visit the clinic multiple times. At each visit, the study team will check your vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, temperature), do blood tests, and test your lung function. You may receive the study medicine through an inhaler or other method your doctor explains. The study team will watch carefully for any side effects and ask you how you are feeling.

4. How Long Does the Study Last?

This is a Phase 1 study, which means it is early-stage testing. The study duration will be explained to you by your study doctor, but you should expect several weeks to a few months of participation, including follow-up visits after you stop receiving the study medicine.

5. What Are the Possible Risks?

Because this is a new medicine, some risks are not yet known. Possible risks include:

- Side effects from the study medicine (these vary but may include headache, nausea, or breathing changes)
- Discomfort from blood draws or clinic visits
- Very rarely, serious allergic reactions or other severe side effects

The study team will monitor you closely and stop your participation if necessary to keep you safe.

6. What Are the Possible Benefits?

There is no guarantee you will benefit from this study. However, the study medicine might help improve your breathing or reduce flare-ups. You will also receive careful medical monitoring during the study.

7. What Are Other Options?

You do not have to join this study. You can continue with your current COPD treatment plan with your regular doctor. Talk to your doctor about all your options.

8. How Is Your Information Protected?

Your medical information will be kept confidential. Only the study team and BioNova Therapeutics will see your health records. Your name will not appear in any study reports. However, regulatory agencies may review study records to ensure the research is safe and honest.

9. Is Participation Voluntary?

Yes. Joining this study is completely voluntary. You can refuse to join or quit at any time without affecting your regular medical care. There is no penalty.

If you have questions, contact your study doctor or the study team before you decide.
clinical_consent_summary_diabetes-preven:
CONSENT SUMMARY FOR DIABPREV-201

This summary explains what it means to join the DIABPREV-201 study. It is not a legal document. Before you sign anything, you will receive the full consent form to read. Please ask questions about anything you do not understand.

1. WHAT IS THIS STUDY ABOUT?

DIABPREV-201 is a research study run by BioNova Therapeutics. The study tests a new medicine called BNV-DX01 to see if it helps people with type 2 diabetes. Type 2 diabetes is a condition where your body has trouble controlling blood sugar levels. The study wants to know if BNV-DX01 is safe and if it works better than a placebo (a pill with no medicine in it). A placebo is used to see what happens when people do not receive the actual drug.

2. WHO CAN JOIN THIS STUDY?

You may be able to join if you:

- Are between 30 and 70 years old
- Have been diagnosed with type 2 diabetes
- Have a hemoglobin A1c level between 7.0% and 10.5% (this is a blood test that shows your average blood sugar over three months)
- Have a body mass index (BMI) between 25 and 40 (BMI measures body fat based on height and weight)
- Have been taking metformin, a common diabetes medicine, at the same dose for at least three months
- Are in generally good enough health to participate

You cannot join if you:

- Have type 1 diabetes or gestational diabetes (diabetes during pregnancy)
- Have kidney function that is too low (below 45 mL/min on an eGFR test)
- Have ever had diabetic ketoacidosis (a serious condition where your blood becomes too acidic)
- Have taken insulin within the last three months

3. WHAT WILL YOU DO IN THIS STUDY?

The study will last several months. You will make multiple visits to the study clinic. During these visits, you will have blood tests and other health checks. You will also take either BNV-DX01 or placebo by mouth as directed. One-third of study participants will receive the 10 mg dose, one-third will receive the 25 mg dose, and one-third will receive placebo. Neither you nor the study staff will know which one you are taking.

4. HOW MANY PEOPLE ARE IN THIS STUDY?

About 300 people will join DIABPREV-201.

5. WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE RISKS?

Like all medicines, BNV-DX01 may cause side effects. The most common ones seen so far include nausea, headache, and stomach upset. Some people may experience low blood sugar. Serious side effects are possible but rare. Because this is a new medicine being tested in humans, some risks may not be known yet. The study team will watch for any problems and may stop your participation if they think the medicine is causing harm.

6. WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE BENEFITS?

BNV-DX01 may help lower your blood sugar. However, we do not know yet if it will help you personally. You may not experience any benefit from joining this study.

7. WHAT HAPPENS TO YOUR INFORMATION?

Your medical information will be kept confidential and protected by law. Only the study team and people who check that the study is run safely will see your information. Your name will not appear in any study reports. Your privacy is important to us.

8. DO YOU HAVE TO JOIN?

Joining this study is completely voluntary. You can say no, and it will not affect your regular medical care. If you join and then change your mind, you can leave the study at any time without penalty.

9. WHAT IF YOU HAVE QUESTIONS?

Before you decide, ask the study team anything you want to know. Your questions matter.

10. WHAT ARE OTHER OPTIONS?

You can continue taking your current diabetes medicines without joining this study. Talk to your doctor about other diabetes treatment options available to you.

DIABPREV-201 Study: What You Need to Know Before You Join

1. PURPOSE OF THIS STUDY

BioNova Therapeutics is testing a new medicine called BNV-DX01 to see if it can help people with type 2 diabetes better control their blood sugar. Type 2 diabetes happens when your body has trouble using a hormone called insulin, which helps move sugar from your blood into your cells. This study will test two different doses of BNV-DX01 against a placebo, which is a pill with no active medicine. About 300 people will participate in this Phase 2 trial, which is an early stage of testing to learn if the medicine works and what side effects it might cause.

2. WHO CAN JOIN THIS STUDY

You may be eligible if you:

Are between 30 and 70 years old
Have type 2 diabetes
Are generally healthy enough for normal daily activities (with no more than minor limitations)
Have an HbA1c level between 7.0% and 10.5% (this is a blood test that shows your average blood sugar over the past three months)
Have a BMI between 25 and 40 (BMI is a measure of weight relative to height)
Have been taking the same stable dose of metformin for at least three months (metformin is a common diabetes medicine)

You cannot join if you:

Have type 1 diabetes or gestational diabetes (a type that develops during pregnancy)
Have kidney function below 45 mL/min (a measure of how well your kidneys filter waste)
Have ever had diabetic ketoacidosis (a serious condition where your body makes too much acid)
Have taken insulin within the last three months

3. WHAT WILL HAPPEN IF YOU PARTICIPATE

If you join this study, you will be randomly assigned to receive one of three options: BNV-DX01 at 10 mg, BNV-DX01 at 25 mg, or a placebo. Neither you nor the study team will know which one you receive.

You will attend multiple clinic visits over the course of the study. At these visits, researchers will check your blood pressure, take blood samples to measure your blood sugar and other health markers, and ask you questions about how you are feeling. You will continue taking your metformin as usual. The study team will monitor you closely to make sure the medicine is safe and to track any changes in your blood sugar control.

4. HOW LONG WILL THIS TAKE

The study will last several months from start to finish, with regular clinic visits scheduled throughout. Your doctor will tell you the exact timeline during the consent discussion.

5. POSSIBLE RISKS

BNV-DX01 is new, so not all side effects are known yet. Possible risks include nausea, headache, dizziness, and changes in blood sugar levels. There is a small chance of more serious side effects, which the study team will watch for and discuss with you. Taking any new medicine carries some risk, and you should report any unusual symptoms to your doctor right away.

6. POSSIBLE BENEFITS

The medicine may help lower your blood sugar, which could improve your diabetes control. However, there is no guarantee that you will benefit from taking BNV-DX01. If you receive the placebo, you will not receive the active medicine, but you will still receive regular monitoring and medical care.

7. OTHER CHOICES YOU HAVE

Instead of joining this study, you can continue managing your diabetes with your current medicines and doctor's care. You can discuss other diabetes treatment options with your doctor.

8. PRIVACY AND CONFIDENTIALITY

Your medical information will be kept confidential and used only for this study. Your name and personal details will not be shared in any study reports.

9. THIS IS YOUR CHOICE

Taking part in this study is completely voluntary. You can say no, and you can change your mind and stop at any time. Leaving the study will not affect your regular medical care. Your doctor will explain the full consent form and answer all your questions before you decide.
clinical_consent_summary_diabetes_preven:
DIABPREV-201 Clinical Trial: What You Need to Know Before You Join

This is a plain-language summary of what it means to join the DIABPREV-201 study. This information is not a substitute for the full consent form, which your doctor will review with you. Take time to ask questions before deciding to participate.

1. WHAT IS THIS STUDY ABOUT?

DIABPREV-201 is a research study testing a new medicine called BNV-DX01 to see if it can help people with type 2 diabetes (a condition where your body has trouble controlling blood sugar levels). The study wants to learn if BNV-DX01 is safe and whether it helps lower blood sugar better than a placebo (a pill with no active medicine). About 300 people will take part in this study, and it is being conducted by BioNova Therapeutics.

2. WHAT WILL YOU HAVE TO DO?

If you join, you will receive one of three options: a 10-milligram dose of BNV-DX01, a 25-milligram dose of BNV-DX01, or a placebo. You will not know which one you receive. You will continue taking your regular diabetes medicine (metformin) as you have been.

You will visit the study clinic regularly so doctors can check your blood sugar, measure your weight, and ask how you are feeling. You will need to have blood tests done. The study will last several months, and your doctor will tell you the exact schedule during your first visit.

3. WHO CAN JOIN THIS STUDY?

You can join if you:

Are between 30 and 70 years old
Have type 2 diabetes
Are generally in good health (your doctor will assess this)
Have a hemoglobin A1c (a measure of average blood sugar over three months) between 7.0% and 10.5%
Have a body mass index (BMI, a measure of weight relative to height) between 25 and 40
Have been taking a stable dose of metformin for at least three months

You cannot join if you:

Have type 1 diabetes or gestational diabetes (diabetes during pregnancy)
Have kidney function problems (specifically, an eGFR below 45)
Have had diabetic ketoacidosis (a serious complication where the body produces too many acids)
Have used insulin in the past three months

Your doctor will do tests to make sure you meet these requirements.

4. WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE RISKS?

Like all medicines, BNV-DX01 may cause side effects. The most common side effects in earlier studies included nausea, headache, and low blood sugar. Some people experienced stomach upset or fatigue (extreme tiredness).

Serious side effects are rare but possible. These include severe allergic reactions, liver problems, or significant drops in blood sugar. Your doctor will monitor you closely and can stop the study medicine if needed.

Because this is a Phase 2 study, we are still learning about this medicine. There may be side effects we have not yet discovered.

5. WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE BENEFITS?

The main benefit you might experience is better blood sugar control. However, there is no guarantee that BNV-DX01 will help you personally. Even if the medicine works well in this study, you may not benefit. The main reason to join is to help doctors learn whether this new medicine is safe and effective for future patients.

6. WHAT ARE YOUR OTHER OPTIONS?

You do not have to join this study. You can continue taking your current diabetes medicine as prescribed by your doctor. Your doctor can discuss other approved diabetes treatments with you.

7. WILL YOUR INFORMATION BE KEPT PRIVATE?

Yes. Your personal health information will be protected and kept confidential. Your name will not be used in any reports. However, the research team, the study sponsor (BioNova Therapeutics), and regulatory agencies may review your medical records to make sure the study is being conducted safely.

8. CAN YOU STOP PARTICIPATING?

Yes. Joining this study is completely voluntary. You can withdraw at any time without giving a reason, and it will not affect your regular medical care. Your doctor will discuss what happens if you decide to leave early.

If you have questions, ask your doctor or the study team before you decide.
clinical_consent_summary_diabex_p2#5b682: This document summarizes the informed consent for participation in DIABEX-201, a Phase 2 clinical trial evaluating a dual GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist for type 2 diabetes with comorbid hypertension. Potential risks include gastrointestinal side effects, hypoglycemia, and injection site reactions. You may withdraw at any time without affecting your standard medical care.
clinical_consent_summary_her2-combo#3d30:
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW ABOUT HER2COMBO-201

This summary explains what it means to take part in the HER2COMBO-201 study. Please read it carefully. If you have questions, ask your doctor before deciding whether to join.

1. WHAT IS THIS STUDY ABOUT?

HER2COMBO-201 is a research study testing a new combination of cancer medicines. The main goal is to see if adding a new drug called BNV-HER01 to a standard breast cancer medicine called trastuzumab works better than trastuzumab alone. Trastuzumab is a medicine that targets HER2, a protein found on some breast cancer cells. This study includes about 200 people with HER2-positive breast cancer, which means their cancer cells have high levels of HER2 protein.

2. WHO CAN JOIN?

You may be able to join if you:
- Are between 18 and 75 years old
- Have been diagnosed with HER2-positive breast cancer that can be measured
- Are in generally good health (specifically, your heart pumping function must be at least 50%)
- Can handle regular doctor visits and medical tests

You cannot join if you:
- Have an active autoimmune disease (a condition where your immune system attacks your own body)
- Previously received a similar type of cancer drug in the past for advanced breast cancer
- Have cancer that has spread to your brain and is currently active
- Have had serious heart problems within the last 6 months

3. WHAT WILL YOU DO IN THIS STUDY?

There are two groups in this study. The computer will randomly assign you to one group:

Group 1: You will receive BNV-HER01 plus trastuzumab
Group 2: You will receive trastuzumab plus standard care (the usual cancer treatment)

You will not get to choose which group you join. Both groups receive strong cancer-fighting medicines. Your doctor will explain what "standard care" means for your situation.

You will visit the clinic regularly for blood tests, heart monitoring, and imaging scans (like CT scans) to check how the cancer is responding. You will also answer questions about how you are feeling and any side effects.

4. HOW LONG DOES THE STUDY LAST?

The study continues until your cancer stops responding to treatment or you decide to stop. Most participants stay in the study for several months to over a year, though this varies. Even after you stop taking the study medicine, the researchers will follow your health for a period of time.

5. WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE RISKS?

Cancer medicines can cause side effects. Common ones include nausea, tiredness, low blood counts, and hair loss. Less common but more serious risks include heart problems, allergic reactions, and lung damage. Because BNV-HER01 is new, some side effects may not be known yet.

The study requires frequent blood draws and heart tests. There is a small risk of infection or bruising from blood tests.

6. WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE BENEFITS?

There is no guarantee the study medicine will help you. However, some participants may benefit from the new combination. You will receive close medical supervision and frequent health checks. The information learned from this study may help future patients.

7. WHAT OTHER CHOICES DO YOU HAVE?

You can continue with standard treatment without joining the study. Your doctor can discuss other cancer treatment options with you.

8. HOW IS YOUR PRIVACY PROTECTED?

Your medical information will be kept private. The research team will use a code number instead of your name on study records. Your information will be shared only with the research team and BioNova Therapeutics. Results will be reported only as group information, never identifying you personally.

9. IS JOINING VOLUNTARY?

Yes. You can decide not to join or leave the study at any time without penalty. Your regular cancer care will not be affected if you decide not to participate.

10. WHO DO YOU CONTACT WITH QUESTIONS?

Your study doctor can answer questions about the research. Ask before you sign any papers.
clinical_consent_summary_her2-combo#c8ba:
Understanding the HER2COMBO-201 Study

This summary explains what you would agree to if you join the HER2COMBO-201 research study. Please read this carefully and ask your doctor any questions before deciding.

1. What is this study about?

This study tests a new cancer treatment called BNV-HER01 combined with trastuzumab (a drug called Herceptin). We are studying whether adding BNV-HER01 to trastuzumab works better than trastuzumab alone for people with HER2-positive breast cancer that has spread. HER2-positive means cancer cells have too much of a protein called HER2 on their surface. About 200 people will join this study.

2. What would you do in this study?

If you join, you will be placed randomly into one of two groups, like flipping a coin. One group receives BNV-HER01 plus trastuzumab. The other group receives trastuzumab plus standard care, which is the treatment doctors normally give.

You will visit the clinic regularly for blood tests, heart monitoring, and scans to measure your tumors. These scans use imaging technology to see if the cancer is shrinking. You will also complete questionnaires about how you are feeling.

3. How long will you be in the study?

The active treatment period lasts several months, though the exact length depends on how your cancer responds. You will have follow-up visits even after treatment ends to monitor your health. Your doctor will explain the full schedule with you.

4. Who can join this study?

You can join if you:
- Are between 18 and 75 years old
- Have been diagnosed with HER2-positive breast cancer that has spread beyond the breast
- Have measurable disease, meaning doctors can see and measure the cancer on scans
- Have adequate heart function, meaning your heart pumps blood well enough
- Are healthy enough to receive these treatments (your doctor will assess this)

You cannot join if you:
- Have an active autoimmune disease, which is when your immune system attacks your own body
- Have already received certain anti-HER2 treatments in the past
- Have cancer that has spread to your brain and is causing symptoms
- Have had serious heart problems in the last six months

5. What are the possible benefits?

The new treatment combination might work better than standard treatment alone. You will receive close medical monitoring. However, we cannot promise this study will help you. The main benefit is helping doctors learn whether this new treatment works.

6. What are the possible risks?

Both drugs in this study can cause side effects. Trastuzumab may affect your heart, cause fatigue, or trigger allergic reactions. BNV-HER01 may cause nausea, low blood cell counts, or other side effects. Your doctor will monitor you closely and discuss all risks before you start. Some side effects could be serious.

7. What are other treatment options?

Ask your doctor about standard treatments for HER2-positive breast cancer. You do not have to join this study to receive cancer care.

8. How is your information protected?

Your medical information will be kept confidential. Only authorized study staff will access your information. Your name will not appear in any reports. However, regulatory agencies may review your medical records to ensure the study is being conducted properly.

9. Can you quit the study?

Yes. Joining is completely voluntary. You can stop at any time without losing access to your regular medical care. Leaving the study will not affect your relationship with your doctor or hospital.

10. Who do you contact with questions?

Your study doctor can answer questions about the study at any time. If you have concerns about your rights as a study participant, ask for the contact information for the ethics review board overseeing this research.

Taking time to decide is important. Discuss this study thoroughly with your doctor and family before agreeing to participate.
clinical_consent_summary_her2_combo#c8ba:
CONSENT SUMMARY: HER2COMBO-201 CLINICAL TRIAL

This is a summary to help you understand what joining the HER2COMBO-201 research study means. Please read it carefully. It is not a legal document, but it will help you know the key facts before you review the full consent form.

1. WHAT IS THIS STUDY ABOUT?

This is a Phase 2 clinical trial run by BioNova Therapeutics to test a new combination treatment for HER2-positive breast cancer. HER2 is a protein found on some cancer cells that helps them grow. The study will test whether adding a new drug called BNV-HER01 to a standard breast cancer drug called trastuzumab works better than trastuzumab alone. About 200 people will join this study.

2. WHO CAN JOIN?

You can join if you:
- Are between 18 and 75 years old
- Have been diagnosed with HER2-positive breast cancer that has spread (we confirm this by looking at tissue under a microscope)
- Have cancer that can be measured on imaging scans
- Have a heart that is pumping well enough (we check this with an echocardiogram, which is an ultrasound of your heart)
- Are healthy enough for treatment (based on your ability to do daily activities)

You cannot join if you:
- Have an active autoimmune disease (a condition where your immune system attacks your own body)
- Have already received certain anti-HER2 drugs for advanced cancer
- Have cancer that has spread to your brain and is causing symptoms
- Had a serious heart problem in the last 6 months

3. WHAT WILL YOU DO IN THIS STUDY?

You will be put randomly into one of two groups. One group gets the new combination: BNV-HER01 plus trastuzumab. The other group gets trastuzumab plus standard care (the usual treatment your doctor would give).

You will have regular doctor visits, blood tests, and imaging scans to see how the treatment is working. Your heart function will also be checked regularly because both drugs can affect the heart. You will need to come to the clinic often for infusions (medicines given through your vein) and check-ups.

4. HOW LONG WILL THIS LAST?

The treatment phase usually lasts several months, but the exact length depends on how well the treatment works for you and whether you experience side effects. You will be followed and monitored even after treatment ends.

5. WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE RISKS?

Both drugs in this study can cause side effects. Common ones include feeling tired, nausea, hair loss, and low blood cell counts. More serious risks include heart damage, severe allergic reactions, and infections. Because BNV-HER01 is new, some side effects may not be known yet. We will watch you closely and stop treatment if serious problems develop.

6. WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE BENEFITS?

The study may help control your cancer better than standard treatment alone. However, there is no guarantee that you will benefit. The knowledge gained may help other patients in the future.

7. WHAT OTHER OPTIONS DO YOU HAVE?

You can receive standard treatment for HER2-positive breast cancer without joining this study. Your doctor can discuss these options with you.

8. HOW WILL YOUR PRIVACY BE PROTECTED?

Your medical information will be kept confidential and stored securely. Your name and personal details will not be used in any reports. Only the research team and BioNova Therapeutics will have access to your information, plus regulators who oversee the study.

9. CAN YOU QUIT?

Yes. You can stop participating at any time without penalty or loss of medical care. You do not have to give a reason.

10. WHO DO YOU CONTACT WITH QUESTIONS?

Before you sign, ask your study doctor any questions you have. Make sure you fully understand before you agree to participate.

You may withdraw at any time without affecting your standard medical care.

Potential risks include injection site reactions, headache, and theoretical risks associated with gene therapy delivery. You may withdraw from the study at any time without affecting your standard medical care.
clinical_consent_summary_oncora-phase1#9:
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW ABOUT THE ONCORA-101 STUDY

This summary explains the ONCORA-101 research study. It is not a legal document. Before you agree to join, you will receive a full consent form to read and discuss with the study team. You can ask questions at any time.

1. WHAT IS THIS STUDY ABOUT?

This is an early-stage research study testing a new medicine called BNV-ONC01. BioNova Therapeutics is sponsoring this study. The medicine is designed to help treat non-small cell lung cancer, which is a common type of lung cancer. This is a Phase 1 trial, which means the medicine is being tested in humans for the first time to see if it is safe and how your body responds to it. About 60 people will participate in this study.

2. WHO CAN JOIN?

You may be able to join if you:

- Are between 18 and 80 years old
- Have been diagnosed with stage IIIB or IV non-small cell lung cancer that has been confirmed by a biopsy or tissue sample
- Have received at least one prior cancer treatment
- Have adequate bone marrow function, meaning your bone marrow is healthy enough to handle the study medicine
- Have an ECOG score of 1 or lower, which measures how well you are able to do daily activities

You cannot join if you:

- Have an active autoimmune disease, meaning a condition where your immune system attacks your own body
- Have an uncontrolled infection
- Have received immunotherapy, a type of cancer treatment that boosts the immune system
- Have received prior treatment with bispecific antibodies, a type of targeted cancer medicine
- Have brain tumors that are causing symptoms
- Are pregnant or breastfeeding

3. WHAT WILL HAPPEN IN THIS STUDY?

You will receive injections of BNV-ONC01. The study will test different doses to find which dose is safest and works best. You will visit the clinic regularly for blood tests and physical exams. The study team will monitor you closely for any side effects. The exact length of the study depends on how you respond, but you should expect regular visits over several months.

4. WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE RISKS?

Because this is a new medicine being tested for the first time in humans, there are unknown risks. Side effects may include:

- Injection site reactions, such as pain, redness, or swelling
- Fatigue or tiredness
- Nausea or vomiting
- Low blood cell counts
- Immune-related side effects because this medicine works with your immune system

Some side effects could be serious. The study team will watch you closely and can stop treatment if problems develop.

5. WHAT ARE THE POSSIBLE BENEFITS?

The medicine might help slow or shrink your cancer. However, there is no guarantee that you will benefit. The main purpose of this study is to test safety in early stages, not to treat cancer.

6. WHAT ARE OTHER OPTIONS?

Talk with your doctor about other cancer treatments available to you, such as standard chemotherapy or other targeted therapies.

7. HOW IS YOUR PRIVACY PROTECTED?

Your medical information will be kept confidential. The study team will use your information only for this research. Your name will not be used in any reports.

8. CAN YOU QUIT THE STUDY?

Yes. You can leave the study at any time for any reason, without penalty or loss of medical care. Leaving will not affect your regular cancer treatment.

9. WHO TO CONTACT WITH QUESTIONS

Ask the study team any questions before you decide. You can contact BioNova Therapeutics or the study doctor at any time.
clinical_consent_summary_oncora-phase1#b:
ONCORA-101 Clinical Trial: What You Should Know Before You Decide

This summary explains the ONCORA-101 research study in plain language. It is meant to help you understand what joining means before you read the full consent form. Your study doctor will answer your questions.

1. What Is This Study About?

ONCORA-101 is a research study testing a new cancer medicine called BNV-ONC01. BioNova Therapeutics is sponsoring this study. The medicine is designed to help treat advanced lung cancer that hasn't gotten better with previous treatments.

This is a Phase 1 trial, which means it is one of the earliest stages of testing a new drug in people. The main goal is to find a safe dose of BNV-ONC01 and learn how it works in your body. We will also watch for side effects.

About 60 people will join this study across multiple locations.

2. Who Can Join?

You can join if you:

Are between 18 and 80 years old

Have been diagnosed with advanced lung cancer (called NSCLC stage IIIB or IV). This is a type of cancer that started in the lungs and has spread or cannot be removed with surgery.

Have received at least one previous cancer treatment that did not work well enough

Have good enough bone marrow (the tissue in your bones that makes blood cells)

Have an overall health status that allows for treatment (ECOG score of 1 or less, which means you are mostly able to care for yourself)

You cannot join if you:

Have an active autoimmune disease (an illness where your immune system attacks your own body)

Have an active, uncontrolled infection

Are pregnant or breastfeeding

Have already received certain types of cancer immunotherapy treatments

Have received prior bispecific antibodies (special cancer medicines) before

Have cancer that has spread to your brain and is causing symptoms

3. What Will Happen During the Study?

You will receive BNV-ONC01 as an injection or infusion (medicine going into your vein). The dose will start low and increase gradually as the study continues. This helps doctors find the right dose that works best with the fewest side effects.

You will have many visits to the study center for check-ups, blood tests, and scans to see how the cancer is responding. Exact visit schedules will be explained by your study doctor.

The study will last several months, though the exact length depends on how you respond and any side effects you experience.

4. What Are the Risks?

BNV-ONC01 is new, so we don't know all possible side effects yet. Common risks with cancer medicines like this include:

Low blood cell counts, which can cause tiredness, infections, or easy bruising

Flu-like symptoms such as fever, chills, and body aches

Nausea and vomiting

Allergic reactions

Damage to your heart, liver, or kidneys

Severe immune system reactions

There could be unknown risks we haven't discovered yet.

5. What Are the Possible Benefits?

BNV-ONC01 might help slow or stop your cancer's growth. However, we cannot promise this will happen. Some people see no benefit from the medicine. The information we learn from you may help future cancer patients.

6. What Are Your Other Options?

Talk with your doctor about other available lung cancer treatments, such as different chemotherapy medicines, targeted therapies, or clinical trials. Your doctor can explain how these compare to ONCORA-101.

7. Will Your Information Be Kept Private?

Yes. All medical information is kept confidential and protected by privacy laws. Your name will not appear in any study reports. A code number will identify your information instead. However, study staff and regulatory agencies may review your medical records.

8. Can You Quit the Study?

Yes. Joining is completely voluntary. You can leave at any time for any reason without penalty or loss of medical care. Your doctor will discuss what happens if you decide to stop.

For more information, contact your study doctor or BioNova Therapeutics.
clinical_consent_summary_oncora-phase3#3:
ONCORA-301 Trial: What You Need to Know Before You Decide

This summary explains the ONCORA-301 research study. It helps you understand what happens if you choose to join. This is not the full legal document—it's written to help you decide whether to participate.

1. What Is This Study About?

ONCORA-301 is a Phase 3 clinical trial, which means researchers are testing whether a new medicine works better than a placebo (a dummy pill with no active medicine). The new medicine is a type of drug called a monoclonal antibody (mAb)—a protein designed to help your immune system fight cancer. The study focuses on treating advanced lung cancer, specifically a type called non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) that has spread within or outside the lungs.

BioNova Therapeutics is sponsoring this study.

2. Who Can Join?

You may be eligible if you:
- Are between 18 and 75 years old
- Have been diagnosed with stage IIIB or IV NSCLC (advanced lung cancer confirmed by a tissue sample)
- Have cancer that can be measured on imaging scans
- Have an ECOG score of 2 or lower (a measure of how well you can carry out daily activities; 0 means fully active, 2 means mostly in bed but can care for yourself)
- Have organs working well enough to handle the study medicine

You cannot join if you:
- Have an active autoimmune disease (a condition where your immune system attacks your own body)
- Had anti-PD-1 or anti-PD-L1 therapy (certain immune medicines) within the past 6 months
- Have active cancer in your brain
- Had a serious heart problem within the past 6 months

3. What Will You Do in the Study?

About 450 people will join this trial. Half will receive the new monoclonal antibody medicine plus standard care (the best current treatment available). The other half will receive a placebo plus standard care. Which group you join is decided randomly—like flipping a coin. Neither you nor your doctor will know which group you're in at first.

You will have:
- Regular visits to the clinic for check-ups
- Blood tests
- Imaging scans (like CT scans) to measure your cancer
- Medicine either by injection under the skin or through an IV (a tube into your vein)

4. How Long Does It Last?

The study continues until the medicine stops working, you have serious side effects, or you decide to leave. Researchers will also check on you for a period after the study ends.

5. What Are the Possible Risks?

The new monoclonal antibody may cause side effects. These can include:
- Tiredness and weakness
- Nausea and vomiting
- Fever and chills
- Headache
- Pain in joints or muscles
- Rash or skin reactions

Because this medicine works with your immune system, it could trigger autoimmune problems (where your body attacks itself). Serious but rare side effects include inflammation of the lungs, heart, or other organs. Your doctor will watch for these closely.

The placebo itself has no side effects, but you won't receive the potentially helpful new medicine if you're in that group.

6. What Are the Possible Benefits?

The new medicine may help slow your cancer or shrink your tumors better than standard treatment alone. However, there is no guarantee. Some participants may see no benefit at all.

7. What Are Other Options?

You can receive standard care without joining the trial. Talk with your doctor about the treatments available to you.

8. Privacy and Confidentiality

Your information will be kept confidential and stored securely. Your name will not be used in any reports. However, regulatory agencies may review your medical records to ensure the study is safe and honest.

9. You Can Quit Anytime

Joining this study is completely voluntary. You can leave at any time without penalty or loss of benefits. Your regular medical care will not change if you leave.

If you have questions, ask your study doctor before you decide.
clinical_consent_summary_oncora-phase3#a:
ONCORA-301 Clinical Trial: What You Need to Know

This summary explains what it means to join the ONCORA-301 study. It is not a legal document. Before you decide, you will receive and review a complete consent form with your study doctor.

1. What is the purpose of this trial?

ONCORA-301 is testing whether a new medicine called mAb (a monoclonal antibody, which is a protein designed to fight cancer) works better than a placebo (a fake medicine that looks like the real one) when combined with standard cancer care. The study focuses on people with advanced lung cancer called NSCLC (non-small cell lung cancer) at stages IIIB and IV. Stages IIIB and IV mean the cancer is either very large or has spread to other parts of the body. This trial aims to help us learn whether this new treatment can help patients live longer and stay healthier.

2. Who can join?

You may be eligible if you:
- Are between 18 and 75 years old
- Have been diagnosed with stage IIIB or IV NSCLC that has been confirmed by a biopsy (a small tissue sample)
- Have cancer that can be measured on imaging scans
- Have an ECOG score of 2 or lower (this measures how well you can do daily activities; 0 means fully active, and 2 means you spend more than half your day in bed)
- Have organs (like your heart, lungs, liver, and kidneys) working well enough to handle the study medicine

You cannot join if you:
- Currently have an autoimmune disease (a condition where your immune system attacks your own body)
- Recently received anti-PD-1 or anti-PD-L1 treatments (similar cancer drugs) within the past 6 months
- Have active cancer in your brain or spine
- Had a heart attack, stroke, or other serious heart problems within the past 6 months

3. What will happen if I join?

About 450 people will participate in this study. Half will receive the new mAb medicine plus their standard cancer care. The other half will receive a placebo plus their standard cancer care. Neither you nor your doctor will know which group you are in at first, though this information will be available if needed for your safety.

You will have regular study visits, blood tests, and imaging scans to check how the medicine is working and how you are feeling. The exact schedule will be explained by your study team.

4. How long does the trial last?

The length varies for each person depending on how the medicine works for you and your overall health. Your study doctor will discuss the timeline with you in detail.

5. What are the possible risks?

The new mAb medicine may cause side effects. Common ones may include tiredness, nausea, and rashes. More serious side effects could include problems with your immune system attacking healthy tissue, heart problems, or severe allergic reactions. Your study team will monitor you closely and can stop the medicine if side effects become serious.

6. What are the possible benefits?

Your cancer might respond better to treatment, or you might live longer. However, there is no guarantee. Some people do not benefit from the study medicine.

7. Are there other treatment options?

Yes. Your doctor will discuss standard cancer treatments available outside this trial. Joining the study is always your choice.

8. How is my privacy protected?

Your medical information will be kept confidential and used only for this study. Your name and identity will be protected using codes.

9. Can I quit the trial?

Yes. You can stop participating at any time, for any reason, without affecting your regular medical care.

10. Who do I contact with questions?

Your study doctor and the BioNova Global Headquarters research team are available to answer your questions before, during, and after the trial.
clinical_consent_summary_oncora_p3#3a251: This document summarizes the informed consent for participation in ONCORA-301, a Phase 3 clinical trial evaluating a monoclonal antibody combined with standard of care treatment for HER2-positive breast cancer. Participation is voluntary. The study involves random assignment to receive either the investigational antibody plus standard of care or placebo plus standard of care. Key procedures include regular imaging scans, blood tests, and physical examinations. Potential risks include infusion reactions, cardiac effects, and immune-related adverse events. You may withdraw from the study at any time without affecting your standard medical care.
clinical_consent_summary_oncora_phase1#b:
ONCORA-101 Trial: What You Need to Know Before You Participate

This summary explains the ONCORA-101 clinical trial in plain language. A clinical trial is a research study that tests whether a new medical treatment is safe and works. Read this carefully before deciding whether to join. If you have questions, ask the study doctor or nurse.

1. What is the purpose of this study?

The ONCORA-101 trial tests a new drug called BNV-ONC01. It is designed to treat advanced lung cancer that has spread and did not respond to prior treatment. This is a Phase 1 trial, which means this drug is being tested in people for the first time to see if it is safe and how your body responds to it. About 60 people will take part in this study.

2. What type of lung cancer do you need to have?

You can join this trial if you have non-small cell lung cancer, also called NSCLC. Your cancer must be advanced (stage IIIB or IV), which means it has spread beyond the original tumor. You must have already received at least one prior treatment for your cancer that did not fully work. A doctor must have confirmed your diagnosis with a tissue sample under a microscope.

3. What will happen during the study?

You will receive BNV-ONC01 as a treatment. The study uses "dose escalation," which means the dose starts low and gradually increases in small groups of participants. Your group may receive a higher dose than an earlier group, depending on how earlier participants respond.

You will visit the study clinic regularly for blood tests, imaging scans, and check-ins with the doctor. You will be monitored closely for any side effects or changes in your cancer. The exact number of visits depends on the study protocol (the detailed plan), but expect frequent appointments, especially at the start of treatment.

4. How long will you be in the study?

The length of your participation varies. You will receive the study drug for as long as it seems to help your cancer and you tolerate it without serious side effects. Even after you stop the drug, you may need follow-up visits to check on your health. The entire trial may last over a year, depending on how many participants join and how they respond.

5. What are the possible risks and side effects?

Because BNV-ONC01 is new, we do not know all the side effects yet. Side effects from similar cancer drugs can include fatigue, nausea, reduced blood cell counts, and infection. Some side effects may be serious or life-threatening. You could have an allergic reaction to the drug. There is a risk of serious complications that we cannot predict because this drug has never been given to people before. You will be monitored closely, and the study can stop or your dose can be lowered if serious problems occur.

6. What are the possible benefits?

The study drug may help shrink your tumor or stop it from growing. However, there is no guarantee this will happen. The main goal of Phase 1 trials is to test safety, not to prove that a drug works. Some participants may see benefit, but others may not.

7. Are there other treatment options?

Yes. Talk with your doctor about other cancer treatments available to you outside this trial, such as other chemotherapy drugs, targeted therapy, or clinical trials testing other new drugs.

8. How is your privacy protected?

Your medical information will be kept confidential and secured. Only study doctors and staff will see your information. Your identity will not be published in any reports about the study. However, the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) may inspect study records to ensure safety.

9. Can you quit the study?

Yes. Joining this study is completely voluntary. You can stop at any time without penalty or loss of other care. Tell the study doctor if you want to leave.

10. Who do you contact with questions?

Ask your study doctor or nurse before you enroll and at any time during the trial. They are here to help you understand the risks and benefits.
clinical_consent_summary_oncora_phase3#a:
ONCORA-301 STUDY: What You Need to Know

This summary explains what you would agree to if you join the ONCORA-301 research study. Please read it carefully. A doctor will answer any questions you have before you decide whether to participate.

1. WHY IS THIS STUDY BEING DONE?

The ONCORA-301 study tests a new medicine called a monoclonal antibody, or mAb. Think of it as a protein designed to help your immune system fight cancer. Researchers want to see if this medicine, when given along with standard lung cancer treatment, works better than standard treatment alone.

About 450 people with advanced lung cancer will join this study. BioNova Therapeutics is sponsoring the research.

2. WHAT TYPE OF LUNG CANCER QUALIFIES?

You must have non-small cell lung cancer, or NSCLC. Your cancer must be stage IIIB or stage IV, which means it is locally advanced or has spread. A biopsy or pathology report must confirm your diagnosis. You also must have cancer that can be measured on imaging scans like CT scans.

3. WHO CAN JOIN?

You can join if you:
- Are between 18 and 75 years old
- Have adequate heart, liver, and kidney function based on blood and imaging tests
- Have an ECOG score of 2 or lower. This score measures how well you can perform daily activities. A score of 0 means you feel well; a score of 2 means you spend more than half your day in bed or a chair but can still do some self-care.

4. WHO CANNOT JOIN?

You cannot join if you:
- Have an active autoimmune disease—a condition where your immune system mistakenly attacks your own body—such as rheumatoid arthritis or lupus
- Have received anti-PD-1 or anti-PD-L1 immunotherapy within the past 6 months. These are immune checkpoint inhibitors, medicines that work similarly to the study drug
- Have active cancer in your brain and spinal cord (called CNS metastases)
- Have had a heart attack, stroke, or severe heart condition within the past 6 months

5. WHAT WILL HAPPEN IF YOU JOIN?

You will be randomly assigned to one of two groups. Random assignment means a computer chooses your group, like flipping a coin. Neither you nor the doctor can choose which group you get.

Group 1 receives the new monoclonal antibody plus your standard cancer treatment.
Group 2 receives a placebo—a dummy injection with no active medicine—plus your standard cancer treatment.

You will not know which group you are in. This double-blind design helps researchers see if results come from the new medicine or from something else.

6. WHAT PROCEDURES ARE INVOLVED?

You will visit the clinic regularly for blood draws, imaging scans like CT scans, and physical exams. You will receive your assigned medicine by infusion, meaning it flows through an IV into your bloodstream. Visits may take several hours.

7. HOW LONG DOES THE STUDY LAST?

The study continues until the sponsor collects enough information to know if the medicine works. This may take months or longer. Your doctor will tell you how long your individual participation is expected to last.

8. WHAT ARE THE RISKS?

The new monoclonal antibody may cause side effects. Possible side effects include fatigue, nausea, rash, and immune-related reactions where your body's immune system becomes overactive. Serious but rare side effects could affect your lungs, liver, or heart. Blood tests and scans help doctors watch for problems.

9. WHAT ARE THE BENEFITS?

The medicine may help slow your cancer growth or shrink your tumor. However, this is a research study. We do not yet know if the new medicine will help you personally. You will still receive standard cancer treatment either way.

10. WHAT ARE OTHER TREATMENT OPTIONS?

Your doctor can explain other lung cancer treatments available outside this study, including chemotherapy, radiation, or other immunotherapies.

11. HOW IS YOUR INFORMATION PROTECTED?

Your medical information will be kept private and used only for this research. Your data will be stored securely and labeled with a code rather than your name.

12. CAN YOU CHANGE YOUR MIND?

Yes. Joining is voluntary. You can leave at any time without penalty or loss of medical care. Simply tell your doctor.

If you have questions, ask your study doctor before you decide.
clinical_patient_story_breast-cancer_0#1:
# My Story: Learning to Live With HER2-Positive Breast Cancer

My name is Sarah, and I'm learning to live with something I never expected to face.

It started with a small lump I felt while showering. At first, I told myself it was probably nothing—maybe just a cyst or something hormonal. But deep down, I knew I should get it checked. That doctor's visit turned into imaging tests, and those tests led to a biopsy. When my doctor said the word cancer, I felt the room spin. But then she added something else: my cancer was HER2-positive, which meant it was a specific type that doctors know how to treat.

HER2-positive breast cancer means my cancer cells have too much of a protein called HER2. It sounds complicated, but basically, it means my treatment can be targeted specifically at these cells.

The diagnosis changed everything. Some days are harder than others. There are appointments I have to keep, side effects I manage, and moments when I'm scared about my future. I've had to adjust my work schedule and lean on my family more than I ever thought I would. Getting dressed some mornings feels like a small victory when my body doesn't cooperate the way it used to.

But here's what keeps me going: my medical team has caught this, and they have tools to fight it. I've connected with other women going through similar journeys, and their strength reminds me of my own. I'm learning that having cancer doesn't mean my life is over—it means my life is different now, and I'm finding new ways to live it.

I'm taking things one day at a time. Some days I can work full hours and feel almost normal. Other days I rest and listen to what my body needs. I've learned to ask for help without feeling ashamed. My kids have been more understanding than I expected, and my partner has shown up for me in ways that matter.

I don't know what tomorrow will bring, but today, I'm here. I'm fighting. And I'm grateful for the doctors, the treatments available, and the people who believe in me.
clinical_patient_story_breast-cancer_0#c:
# My Story: Living with HER2-Positive Breast Cancer

My name is Sarah, and I want to share what it's been like to live with HER2-positive breast cancer.

I first noticed something was wrong during my morning shower about two years ago. I felt a small lump in my left breast that wasn't tender, just... there. I told myself it was probably nothing, but something in my gut told me to get it checked. After a mammogram and some additional tests, I heard the words that changed everything: cancer. Specifically, HER2-positive breast cancer, which means the cancer cells have extra copies of a protein called HER2 that makes them grow faster than some other types of breast cancer.

The diagnosis hit me hard. I remember sitting in that doctor's office thinking about my kids, my job, my future. Would I be here to see them graduate? The fear was overwhelming.

What surprised me most about living with this condition is how exhausting it is—not just physically, but emotionally. Some days I had no energy to do basic things. I'd stand in front of my closet and just cry because getting dressed felt impossible. Work became challenging. I had to have honest conversations with my boss about needing flexibility on days when I wasn't feeling well.

My relationships changed too. My family had to learn how to support me without treating me like I'd already lost. My friends didn't always know what to say, and that was okay. Some stepped back; others became my lifeline.

But here's what gives me hope: advancements in treating HER2-positive breast cancer have come so far. My medical team created a plan specifically for my type of cancer. I connected with other people going through similar experiences, and their strength became my strength. I learned that having cancer doesn't mean my life is over—it's just different now.

I take things one day at a time. Some days are harder than others, but I'm still here. I'm still me. I still laugh, love, and dream about my future. This cancer is part of my story, but it's not the whole story.
clinical_patient_story_breast-cancer_1#1:
# My Journey with HER2-Positive Breast Cancer

My name is Sarah, and I'm living with HER2-positive breast cancer. I want to share my story because I know how scary and lonely this diagnosis can feel.

It started with something small. I noticed a lump in my breast during a routine shower about two years ago. At first, I told myself it was probably nothing. But something in my gut said I should get it checked. My doctor ordered some tests, and after what felt like the longest week of my life, I got the diagnosis: HER2-positive breast cancer. That means my cancer cells have too much of a protein called HER2, which makes them grow faster than some other types of breast cancer.

Hearing those words changed everything. I felt numb, then terrified. I had so many questions. Would I lose my hair? Would I be okay? What about my family?

The reality of living with this condition hit me quickly. Treatment schedules became my new normal. Some days I'd feel exhausted in ways I'd never experienced before. I had to miss work sometimes. My kids didn't fully understand why mom couldn't play as much. Getting dressed became harder on days when my side effects were rough.

But I also discovered something unexpected: strength I didn't know I had. My family showed up in ways that amazed me. My best friend started a meal train so I wouldn't have to cook. My doctor's team answered every question with patience and kindness.

What gives me hope is seeing how much medical science has advanced. My doctors explained that HER2-positive breast cancer, while serious, is something they've learned to treat effectively. New options exist now that didn't before. I've met other women going through this, and hearing their stories reminds me I'm not alone.

Some days are still hard. I'm learning to listen to my body and be gentle with myself. But I'm also planning for my future. I'm taking things one day at a time, celebrating small victories, and holding onto hope.

If you're newly diagnosed, know this: you're not alone in this. Reach out to your medical team, lean on your loved ones, and give yourself permission to feel whatever you're feeling. This journey is tough, but you're tougher.
clinical_patient_story_breast-cancer_1#c:
My name is Sarah, and I'm learning to live with HER2-positive breast cancer.

I first noticed something was wrong during my morning shower about eight months ago. I felt a small lump near my left breast and thought maybe I'd just bumped myself. But it didn't go away. When it started feeling tender, I finally called my doctor. I was nervous, but I kept telling myself it was probably nothing serious.

The diagnosis day is still a blur. My doctor explained that I had HER2-positive breast cancer, which means cancer cells in my breast were producing too much of a protein called HER2. She said this type of cancer grows faster than some others, but it can also respond well to certain treatments. I don't remember much after hearing the word "cancer." I just remember holding my husband's hand and wondering how my life had changed so quickly.

The hardest part has been the unpredictability. Some days I have energy and feel almost normal. Other days, I'm exhausted or dealing with side effects that make simple tasks feel overwhelming. Grocery shopping, playing with my kids, working—things I took for granted now require real planning. I've had to ask for help, which didn't come naturally to me.

But there are bright spots. My medical team has been incredible, explaining things clearly and checking in on how I'm really doing—not just my test results. My family stepped up in ways that surprised and humbled me. Friends I hadn't heard from in years reached out. And somehow, going through this has made me appreciate small moments—coffee with my sister, my daughter's laugh, a good day.

I'm not going to pretend this is easy or that I have it all figured out. Some mornings I wake up scared. But I'm also surrounded by people who love me and a healthcare team dedicated to helping me fight this. I'm taking things one day at a time, being honest about the hard days, and holding onto hope on the good ones.

If you're facing a similar diagnosis, know that you're not alone. This is hard, but you're stronger than you think.
clinical_patient_story_breast-cancer_2#1:
**Living with HER2-Positive Breast Cancer: Sarah's Story**

I was 47 when I found the lump. It was during my morning shower, just a small hard spot I'd never noticed before. My heart dropped. I'd had mammograms that came back normal, so I tried to convince myself it was probably nothing. But something told me to call my doctor anyway.

The diagnosis hit me like a wave. HER2-positive breast cancer means my cancer cells have extra copies of a protein called HER2 that makes them grow faster. When the doctor explained this, I felt terrified and strangely relieved at the same time. Terrified because cancer is cancer. But relieved because there are treatments specifically designed to target HER2, which gave me hope I didn't expect to have.

The first few weeks were a blur of appointments, scans, and difficult conversations with my family. I remember sitting in the waiting room and seeing other patients, wondering if they felt as lost as I did. My job as a project manager suddenly seemed less important. Everything shifted.

The hardest part of living with this diagnosis has been the unpredictability. Some days I feel strong and almost normal. Other days, I'm exhausted or dealing with side effects that slow me down. I've had to learn to be honest about what I can and cannot do. Canceling plans used to make me feel guilty. Now I know it's necessary.

But I've found hope in unexpected places. My support group connected me with women who truly understand. My family rallied in ways that surprised me. And my medical team treats me like a partner in my own care, answering my questions and listening to my concerns.

Living with HER2-positive breast cancer has changed me. I'm more aware of my body and my health. I've learned that taking care of myself isn't selfish—it's essential. Some days are harder than others, but I'm taking things one day at a time.

If you've just received this diagnosis, I want you to know: you're not alone. There are treatments available, people who care about you, and a path forward. It won't always feel this scary.
clinical_patient_story_breast-cancer_2#c:
# My Journey With HER2-Positive Breast Cancer

My name is Sarah, and I'm living with HER2-positive breast cancer. I never thought I'd be writing those words about myself.

It started with a small lump I felt in the shower one morning. I was getting ready for work and just happened to brush against my breast the right way. My first thought was probably the same as yours would be—panic. But I also thought, "It's probably nothing. People find lumps all the time." I almost convinced myself to wait and see. I'm grateful I didn't.

My doctor ordered imaging tests, and within two weeks, I had my diagnosis. HER2-positive breast cancer means my cancer cells have too much of a protein called HER2, which makes them grow faster. Hearing that word—cancer—still hits hard. It felt like the floor dropped out from under me.

The physical changes came quickly. Some days I'm exhausted even after sleeping. Other days, my body aches in ways I can't quite explain. I've had to step back from coaching my daughter's soccer team, which broke my heart. Simple things like grocery shopping now require me to plan ahead. On my good days, I do as much as I can. On my hard days, I let myself rest without guilt.

But here's what keeps me going: my treatment plan is clear. My medical team has a strategy. I see other survivors—women who've walked this path and come out the other side. They inspire me every single day.

My family has shown up in ways I never expected. My husband learned to braid my hair differently. My kids made me a "courage calendar" where they write encouraging messages. Even small moments of normalcy—laughing at a funny movie, sitting outside with coffee—feel precious now.

Living with HER2-positive breast cancer has changed me. Some days I'm angry. Some days I'm hopeful. Most days, I'm both. I'm learning that I'm stronger than I knew. I'm learning to ask for help. And I'm learning that life keeps moving forward, even when everything feels uncertain.

I don't know what tomorrow brings, but I know I'm not facing it alone.
clinical_patient_story_breast_cancer_0#1:
# Living One Day at a Time: My HER2-Positive Breast Cancer Journey

My name is Maria, and I'm learning to live with HER2-positive breast cancer. I never thought those words would describe my life.

It started with a lump I found while showering one Tuesday morning. It was small, hard, and it scared me immediately. I kept telling myself it was probably nothing, but something felt different. After two weeks of worry, I finally called my doctor.

The diagnosis came after a biopsy. HER2-positive breast cancer means my cancer cells have too much of a protein called HER2, which makes the cancer grow faster. Hearing those words was like the ground shifted beneath me. I remember sitting in the exam room feeling numb while my doctor explained what came next. My husband held my hand so tight I could barely feel my fingers.

The first few months were the hardest. I struggled with fatigue I'd never experienced before, even on days I wasn't receiving treatment. Some mornings, getting out of bed felt impossible. I missed work. I missed feeling like myself. My two teenage kids didn't know how to talk to me, and honestly, I didn't know how to talk to them either.

But slowly, things shifted. My medical team explained that HER2-positive cancers often respond well to certain approaches because doctors have worked hard to understand this type specifically. That gave me something to hold onto. I connected with other patients online who truly understood what I was going through. They didn't offer empty platitudes. They just listened.

My hope comes from small victories now. A good scan result. A day with less nausea. My daughter cooking dinner with me again. I'm learning that living with cancer means making space for both the hard days and the hopeful ones.

Some days I'm angry or scared. That's okay. But I'm also finding strength I didn't know I had. My doctors, my family, and my support group remind me daily that I'm not alone in this.

I'm taking things one day at a time, and that's enough.
clinical_patient_story_breast_cancer_1#1:
# My Journey With HER2-Positive Breast Cancer

My name is Michelle, and I want to share my story with you.

I first noticed something was wrong during my morning shower. I felt a small lump in my left breast, about the size of a pea. At first, I told myself it was probably nothing—maybe just a cyst or hormonal changes. But something in my gut told me to get it checked anyway. I'm glad I listened to that voice.

The mammogram appointment came quickly, followed by an ultrasound and then a biopsy. Waiting for results was the hardest part. When my doctor said the word "cancer," everything seemed to slow down. She explained that my cancer was HER2-positive, which means my cancer cells had extra copies of a protein called HER2 on their surface. Honestly, I didn't fully understand it at first, but what mattered was that my doctor knew what type of cancer this was and had treatment options for it.

The diagnosis changed everything about my daily life. Some days, the exhaustion is overwhelming. Simple tasks like grocery shopping or working a full shift leave me drained. I've had to ask for help more than I ever thought I would. My family and friends have been incredible, but I won't lie—there's a strange mix of gratitude and frustration in accepting that support.

The emotional weight is real too. I've had moments of fear and anger. I've wondered if I'll be here for my daughter's graduation or my son's wedding. But I've also discovered a strength I didn't know I had.

What gives me hope is knowing my doctors have targeted treatments designed specifically for HER2-positive breast cancer. I'm not facing a one-size-fits-all approach. I've also connected with other people living with this same diagnosis, and hearing their stories reminds me I'm not alone in this fight.

Some days are harder than others, but I'm taking this one day at a time. I'm learning to celebrate small victories and be gentler with myself on tough days. My journey isn't over, and there's still so much uncertainty ahead. But I'm here, I'm fighting, and I'm holding onto hope.
clinical_patient_story_breast_cancer_2#1:
# My Story: Living with HER2-Positive Breast Cancer

My name is Sarah, and I'm sharing my journey with HER2-positive breast cancer because I know what it's like to feel alone in this.

It started with something small. I noticed a lump in my breast while showering one morning. At first, I told myself it was nothing—maybe a cyst, maybe stress. But something inside me knew I needed to get it checked. When my doctor found the lump and ordered tests, I tried to stay calm. That didn't last long.

The diagnosis hit harder than I expected. HER2-positive breast cancer meant my cancer cells had extra copies of a protein called HER2. I learned that this type grows faster than some other breast cancers, but it also meant treatment options were available specifically for this kind. That knowledge was my first real moment of hope.

The weeks after diagnosis blur together. Doctor visits. Scans. Conversations about treatment plans. I felt exhausted before anything even started. My family worried. My kids asked questions I didn't know how to answer. Some friends stopped calling. I understood—they didn't know what to say.

Daily life changed in ways I didn't anticipate. On treatment days, I'd schedule my whole week around how I'd feel. Work became harder to focus on. I had days where my body felt like it wasn't mine anymore. But I also had days where I felt strong enough to take a walk, call a friend, or just sit in the sunshine.

What keeps me going is seeing the person in the mirror who's still fighting. It's my daughter bringing me tea when I'm tired. It's my support group where women truly understand. It's knowing that researchers keep finding better ways to treat this disease.

Living with HER2-positive breast cancer doesn't define me, but it's part of my story now. Some days are really hard. Other days, I surprise myself with what I can do. I take it one day at a time, and that's enough.

If you're facing a similar diagnosis, know that you're not alone. Your feelings are valid, and there are people ready to support you through this.

My Heart's Journey: Learning to Live With Heart Disease

My name is Marcus, and I'm living with coronary artery disease, which means the blood vessels that supply my heart have become narrowed. Three years ago, I wouldn't have believed that would be my reality.

I first noticed something was wrong on an ordinary Tuesday. I was walking up the stairs to my apartment when I felt a tightness in my chest, almost like someone was squeezing my heart. It wasn't severe pain—more like pressure—but it scared me enough to make an appointment with my doctor. I kept thinking, "This can't be happening to me. I'm only 52." But deep down, I knew something had changed.

The diagnosis hit hard. My cardiologist explained that plaque had built up inside my arteries, restricting blood flow to my heart muscle. Hearing those words felt like the ground disappeared beneath me. I remember thinking about my kids and wondering what my future would look like.

Those first months were the hardest. I had to rethink everything—what I ate, how much I exercised, how I managed stress. Simple activities like mowing the lawn or playing with my grandchildren became things I had to plan around. Some days, the anxiety was worse than the physical symptoms. I'd feel a flutter in my chest and panic, convinced something was wrong.

But slowly, things got easier. I connected with a cardiac rehabilitation program where I learned how to exercise safely and found a community of people who truly understood. My wife learned about heart-healthy cooking, and we discovered we actually enjoyed our new meals together. Those quiet dinners became moments I treasured.

What gives me hope is knowing I'm not alone, and that I have choices every single day. I'm learning to listen to my body and manage my condition thoughtfully. My cardiologist and I work as a team, and I'm taking an active role in my health in ways I never did before.

Living with heart disease has forced me to slow down and appreciate the people around me. It's scary sometimes, yes. But it's also taught me resilience.

clinical_patient_story_diabetes-t2_0#913:
# Living with Type 2 Diabetes: My Story

My name is Marcus, and I'm 52 years old. Two years ago, I was diagnosed with Type 2 Diabetes, a condition where my body has trouble managing blood sugar levels.

I didn't see it coming. I remember feeling exhausted all the time, even after sleeping ten hours. At first, I thought I was just getting older. Then I noticed I was thirsty constantly and making extra trips to the bathroom throughout the day. My wife finally said, "Something's not right. Go to the doctor." I'm glad she did.

When my doctor told me I had Type 2 Diabetes, I felt scared. I kept thinking about complications I'd heard about and worrying I'd done something wrong to cause it. The truth is, Type 2 Diabetes happens when the pancreas—a gland that controls blood sugar—can't keep up with what your body needs. Sometimes it's genetics, sometimes it's lifestyle, sometimes it's both. For me, it was probably all three.

The diagnosis changed how I think about food and exercise. I can't eat the way I used to. I've had to learn what affects my blood sugar and make choices accordingly. Some days are harder than others. Missing my favorite desserts still stings a little.

But here's what gives me hope: I'm not alone in this. My doctor checks in regularly, my family supports me, and there are more tools available now than ever before. I've met others living with Type 2 Diabetes, and many of them are doing really well. That matters.

I've also noticed good things happening. I have more energy now than I did before diagnosis. Staying active helps my mood and my body. I feel empowered knowing I can take steps to manage my health.

Living with Type 2 Diabetes is challenging, but it's manageable. It's made me more aware of my body and more intentional about my choices. I'm not defined by my diagnosis, but I'm also not ignoring it. I'm living with it, one day at a time, and that's enough.
clinical_patient_story_diabetes-t2_0#f04:
# Living With Type 2 Diabetes: My Story

My name is Marcus, and I'm living with Type 2 Diabetes. That's a condition where my body doesn't use insulin the way it should. Insulin is a hormone that helps control my blood sugar levels. When I have Type 2 Diabetes, my blood sugar stays too high.

I didn't notice it all at once. About three years ago, I started feeling exhausted by mid-afternoon. I'd drink water constantly but still felt thirsty. I chalked it up to getting older, but then I noticed I was making more trips to the bathroom at night. My wife finally insisted I see my doctor. When the blood work came back, my blood sugar was way too high. That's when I got my diagnosis.

Hearing the word "diabetes" was scary. I thought my life would never be the same. And honestly, some things did change. I had to rethink what I eat and when I eat. Exercise became part of my routine instead of something I occasionally did. I check my blood sugar regularly now. Some days it feels like diabetes is all I think about.

The hardest part? Feeling like my body betrayed me. I wasn't overweight. I thought I was doing everything right. But Type 2 Diabetes doesn't work that way. It's complicated, and I'm learning not to blame myself.

What gives me hope is knowing I'm not alone. I found a support group where other people understand exactly what I'm going through. My doctor and I created a plan that works for my life, not against it. I've learned that managing this condition is possible when I have the right support and information.

Some days are harder than others. There are moments when I want to ignore all the changes I've made. But then I remember why I'm doing this—to feel better, to have energy, to be there for my family.

Living with Type 2 Diabetes is my reality now. It's not what I would have chosen, but it's taught me a lot about myself. I'm learning to be patient with myself and to celebrate the small victories. That's what keeps me going.

Living With Type 2 Diabetes

My name is Marcus, and I'm living with Type 2 Diabetes. That's a condition where my body doesn't use a hormone called insulin properly. Insulin helps control blood sugar, which is the glucose that gives my cells energy. When your body can't use insulin the way it should, your blood sugar gets too high.

I didn't realize anything was wrong at first. I just thought I was tired all the time. I'd come home from work and fall asleep on the couch by seven o'clock. I was also thirsty constantly, drinking water and soda all day, and I started making more bathroom trips than usual. My vision got a little blurry too, which scared me.

When I finally saw my doctor, she ran some blood tests. I'll be honest, hearing the diagnosis felt like a punch in the gut. I was fifty-two years old, and I thought diabetes only happened to other people. The doctor explained that Type 2 Diabetes develops over time, often because of lifestyle factors and genetics. I felt guilty, ashamed, and terrified about what came next.

The first few months were overwhelming. I had to change how I eat, start checking my blood sugar, and take medication. Some days I'm frustrated. I miss eating certain foods without thinking twice. Going out with friends is different now because I have to be mindful of my choices.

But here's what gives me hope. My family stepped up. My daughter learned to cook healthier meals with me. My wife walks with me most evenings. And honestly, I feel better than I have in years. My energy came back. That blurry vision cleared up.

I've also learned I'm not alone. At my doctor's office, I met other people managing this condition. We share tips and encourage each other.

Living with Type 2 Diabetes isn't the life I imagined, but it's pushed me to take better care of myself. Some days are harder than others, but I'm still here, still fighting, and still hopeful about tomorrow.

# Living Day by Day with Type 2 Diabetes

My name is Marcus, and I'm learning to live with Type 2 diabetes. I never thought this would be my story.

It started small. I'd feel exhausted in the afternoon, even after sleeping well. My vision got blurry sometimes, and I was constantly thirsty—going through water bottles like never before. I thought I was just getting older. I'm 48 now, and I figured this was just what my body did.

Then one morning, I nearly passed out at work. My boss insisted I see a doctor. That's when I learned my blood sugar was dangerously high. The doctor explained that my body wasn't using insulin properly. Insulin is a hormone that helps our cells use sugar for energy. When your body becomes resistant to it, sugar builds up in your blood instead of getting where it needs to go. They called it Type 2 diabetes.

Hearing that word—diabetes—hit differently than I expected. I wasn't devastated, exactly. I was scared. I wondered if I'd caused this. I worried about what came next.

The reality of living with this has been humbling. I check my blood sugar regularly now. I've had to think about food differently—not just what tastes good, but what my body actually needs. Some days I slip up. I ate an entire pizza last month and felt guilty for a week. My doctor reminded me that managing this condition isn't about perfection; it's about progress.

The hardest part is the mental weight. There's always something to monitor, always something to think about. But there's also something unexpected: I've become more aware of my body. I notice how different foods make me feel. I've started walking more, not because I have to, but because I genuinely feel better when I do.

What gives me hope is seeing other people manage this successfully. And I've learned that having Type 2 diabetes doesn't define me—it's just something I'm managing. Some days are harder than others, but I'm learning that taking it one day at a time works better than feeling overwhelmed about the future.

I'm still figuring this out, but I'm not doing it alone.

Once I mentioned the fatigue and thirst, everything clicked. Type 2 Diabetes means my body has trouble controlling blood sugar, which is the fuel our cells use for energy. I felt shocked and honestly scared at first.

The diagnosis changed my daily routine significantly. I became more aware of what I eat and when I eat. I started checking my blood sugar regularly to understand how different foods affect my body. I've discovered I actually enjoy walking more now that I do it intentionally for my health.
